> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT863 - PROBABILISTIC ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problem Sets on Probability and Statistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Probability** is the mathematical framework for quantifying uncertainty — assigning a numerical value in the range **[0, 1]** to the likelihood of occurrence of a random event. **Statistics** is the discipline of collecting, analyzing, and interpreting data subject to variability, enabling inference about underlying probabilistic models.

In the context of **Probabilistic Robotics**, probability and statistics form the *backbone* of how a robot reasons about its own state and the world. Sensors are noisy, actuators are imperfect, and environments are partially observable — so the robot must treat measurements and beliefs as **random variables** governed by probability distributions.

The fundamental objects we work with in this module are:

- **Random Variable $X$** — A variable whose value is subject to randomness (e.g., a robot's position estimate).
- **Probability Density Function (PDF) $p(x)$** — Describes the relative likelihood that $X$ takes a specific value $x$. For continuous variables, $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(x) \, dx = 1$.
- **Probability Mass Function (PMF) $P(X = x_i)$** — The discrete counterpart of the PDF.
- **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$**.
- **Joint Probability $P(X=x, Y=y)$** — Probability of two events co-occurring.
- **Conditional Probability $P(X \mid Y)$** — Probability of $X$ given that $Y$ has occurred.
- **Marginal Probability** — Obtained by summing/integrating out nuisance variables.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The Noisy GPS Analogy:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a city and your friend calls out your location over a crackly phone. You are *almost certain* you are near the coffee shop, but the static could be from one block over. A probabilistic robot is exactly in this situation every millisecond: it hears a *noisy* sensor reading and must reason about *where* it is with *some* uncertainty. Probability is the math of being "almost sure." Statistics is the science of getting better at "almost sure" by collecting more noisy evidence.

### 1.3 Why Probability & Statistics are Non-Negotiable in Robotics

- **Sensor Noise:** Every real sensor (LiDAR, camera, IMU) returns measurements corrupted by noise — modelled by probability distributions.
- **Actuator Error:** Wheel encoders, motor commands do not produce deterministic motion.
- **Partial Observability:** Robots cannot directly observe their pose, only infer it.
- **Data-Driven Learning:** Statistical estimators (mean, variance, MAP, MLE) extract information from raw data.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember (highlighted for KTU exams):**
> - **Mean (Expectation):** $\mu = E[X]$ — center of mass of the distribution.
> - **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = E[(X-\mu)^2]$ — spread of the distribution.
> - **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ — average deviation from mean.
> - **Covariance:** $\Sigma_{XY} = E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]$ — joint variability.
> - **Correlation Coefficient:** $\rho \in [-1, 1]$ — normalized covariance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Probability Axioms (Kolmogorov Axioms)

For any events $A$ and $B$ in a sample space $\Omega$:

1. **Non-negativity:** $P(A) \geq 0$
2. **Normalization:** $P(\Omega) = 1$
3. **Additivity (countable):** $P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$ for disjoint events.

### 2.2 Core Conditional Probability Framework

The **conditional probability** of $A$ given $B$ (with $P(B) > 0$) is:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

**The Product Rule (Chain Rule):**

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \, P(B) = P(B \mid A) \, P(A)$$

**Total Probability Theorem:** If $\{B_i\}$ is a partition of the sample space:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i} P(A \mid B_i) \, P(B_i)$$

### 2.3 Bayes' Theorem — The Heart of Probabilistic Robotics

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}$$

In robotics notation (with $x$ = state, $z$ = measurement):

$$P(x \mid z) = \frac{P(z \mid x) \, P(x)}{P(z)}$$

- $P(x)$ — **Prior** belief about state.
- $P(z \mid x)$ — **Likelihood** of measurement given the state (sensor model).
- $P(x \mid z)$ — **Posterior** belief (what we want to compute).
- $P(z)$ — **Evidence** (normalizing constant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bayes' Rule is sacred in robotics:** It is the *only* mathematically consistent way to *invert* a sensor model — converting "what would the sensor see if I were at state $x$?" into "what state am I likely in, given what my sensor actually saw?"

### 2.4 Independence and Conditional Independence

- **$A$ and $B$ are independent** if $P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B)$.
- **$A$ and $B$ are conditionally independent given $C$** if $P(A \cap B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) \, P(B \mid C)$.

This property is exploited heavily in Bayesian networks and SLAM factor graphs.

### 2.5 Expectation, Variance, and Moments

For a continuous random variable $X$ with PDF $p(x)$:

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, p(x) \, dx$$

$$E[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x) \, p(x) \, dx$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = \sigma^2$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Sensor measurement likelihoods |
| Bayes' Rule | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Localization, SLAM posterior update |
| Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_{i} P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ | Multi-hypothesis tracking |
| Expectation (Discrete) | $E[X] = \sum_i x_i P(x_i)$ | Mean position estimate |
| Expectation (Continuous) | $E[X] = \int x \, p(x) \, dx$ | Mean of continuous noise |
| Variance | $\sigma^2 = E[X^2] - \mu^2$ | Uncertainty quantification |
| Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | $1\sigma$, $2\sigma$ confidence intervals |
| Covariance | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]$ | Multi-sensor fusion (Kalman Filter) |
| Gaussian PDF | $p(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Additive white noise model |
| Multivariate Gaussian | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert \Sigma \vert}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | Full robot pose $(x,y,\theta)$ uncertainty |
| 68-95-99.7 Rule | Within $1\sigma$ → 68.27%, $2\sigma$ → 95.45%, $3\sigma$ → 99.73% | Outlier rejection thresholds |
| Law of Total Expectation | $E[X] = E[E[X \mid Y]]$ | Hierarchical estimation |
| Markov Property | $P(X_t \mid X_{0:t-1}) = P(X_t \mid X_{t-1})$ | Recursive Bayesian filtering |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** The Gaussian distribution is the workhorse of robotics because (a) the Central Limit Theorem justifies it for sums of many small noise sources, (b) it is the *maximum entropy* distribution for a given mean and variance, and (c) it yields closed-form updates in the Kalman Filter.

### 2.7 The Three Pillars of a Probabilistic Robotics Problem

1. **State Estimation** — Given noisy measurements, what is the robot's belief about its pose $x_t$? → Recursive Bayes Filter.
2. **Parameter Estimation** — Fit a distribution (e.g., Gaussian) to observed data → MLE, MAP.
3. **Hypothesis Testing** — Which of two models generated this data? → Likelihood ratio tests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Solved Problem Sets)

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked-out problems cover the most commonly tested probability/statistics patterns for KTU Module 1. Each step is shown in full.

---

### PROBLEM 3.1 — Bayes' Theorem with Sensor Noise

**Statement:** A mobile robot uses a wall-mounted landmark detector. The sensor has the following characteristics:
- Prior probability of being near a landmark: $P(L) = 0.2$
- Probability of detection given landmark present: $P(+\mid L) = 0.95$
- Probability of false positive: $P(+\mid \neg L) = 0.05$

If the sensor returns a positive detection, find $P(L \mid +)$ (probability that a landmark is actually present).

**Step 1: Identify knowns and unknown.**

We need the posterior $P(L \mid +)$. We are given:
- Prior: $P(L) = 0.2 \Rightarrow P(\neg L) = 0.8$
- Sensitivity (true positive rate): $P(+\mid L) = 0.95$
- False positive rate: $P(+\mid \neg L) = 0.05$

**Step 2: Write Bayes' Theorem.**

$$P(L \mid +) = \frac{P(+\mid L) \, P(L)}{P(+)}$$

**Step 3: Compute the evidence $P(+)$ using Total Probability.**

$$P(+) = P(+\mid L) P(L) + P(+\mid \neg L) P(\neg L)$$

$$P(+) = (0.95)(0.2) + (0.05)(0.8)$$

$$P(+) = 0.19 + 0.04 = 0.23$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into Bayes' formula.**

$$P(L \mid +) = \frac{(0.95)(0.2)}{0.23} = \frac{0.19}{0.23}$$

$$\boxed{P(L \mid +) \approx 0.8261}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

Even with a sensor that has 95% sensitivity, the posterior probability of a true landmark is only **82.6%** due to the low base rate (prior). This is the **base-rate fallacy**, and it is one of the most important insights in probabilistic sensor fusion.

---

### PROBLEM 3.2 — Multivariate Gaussian Robot Pose

**Statement:** A robot's 2D pose is estimated as a bivariate Gaussian:
$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.09 \end{bmatrix}$$

(a) Compute the PDF value at point $\mathbf{x} = (2.1, 1.2)^T$.
(b) Compute the Mahalanobis distance $d^2 = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$.
(c) Comment on the correlation between $x$ and $y$.

**Step 1: Compute the determinant of $\Sigma$.**

$$\vert \Sigma \vert = (0.04)(0.09) - (0.01)^2 = 0.0036 - 0.0001 = 0.0035$$

**Step 2: Compute $\Sigma^{-1}$.**

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix:
$$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{\vert \Sigma \vert} \begin{bmatrix} 0.09 & -0.01 \\ -0.01 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{0.0035} \begin{bmatrix} 0.09 & -0.01 \\ -0.01 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 25.714 & -2.857 \\ -2.857 & 11.429 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Form the difference vector.**

$$\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 - 2.0 \\ 1.2 - 1.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute Mahalanobis distance (b).**

$$d^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 25.714 & -2.857 \\ -2.857 & 11.429 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

First, compute the middle product:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 25.714 & -2.857 \\ -2.857 & 11.429 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5714 - 0.5714 \\ -0.2857 + 2.2858 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0000 \\ 2.0001 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now, the full product:
$$d^2 = (0.1)(2.0000) + (0.2)(2.0001) = 0.20 + 0.40002 = 0.60002$$

$$\boxed{d^2 \approx 0.60}$$

**Step 5: Compute the PDF (a).**

For a 2D Gaussian ($n=2$):
$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{\vert \Sigma \vert}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2} d^2\right)$$

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{0.0035}} \exp(-0.30)$$

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2\pi (0.05916)} \cdot 0.7408$$

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{0.7408}{0.3717} \approx 1.993$$

$$\boxed{p(\mathbf{x}) \approx 1.99}$$

**Step 6: Compute correlation coefficient (c).**

$$\rho = \frac{\Sigma_{12}}{\sigma_x \sigma_y} = \frac{0.01}{\sqrt{0.04}\sqrt{0.09}} = \frac{0.01}{(0.2)(0.3)} = \frac{0.01}{0.06}$$

$$\boxed{\rho = 0.1667}$$

Since $\rho \neq 0$, the $x$ and $y$ coordinates are **weakly positively correlated** — the off-diagonal covariance couples them.

---

### PROBLEM 3.3 — Law of Total Expectation in Hierarchical Estimation

**Statement:** A robot estimates the distance to a wall using two cascaded sensors. The first sensor produces an estimate $Y$ whose mean depends on the true distance $X$ via $E[Y \mid X] = 0.9X$ with variance $\text{Var}(Y \mid X) = 4$. The true distance $X$ has mean $\mu_X = 10$ and variance $\sigma_X^2 = 2$. Find $E[Y]$ and $\text{Var}(Y)$.

**Step 1: Apply Law of Total Expectation.**

$$E[Y] = E[E[Y \mid X]] = E[0.9X] = 0.9 \, E[X] = 0.9(10) = 9$$

**Step 2: Apply Law of Total Variance.**

$$\text{Var}(Y) = E[\text{Var}(Y \mid X)] + \text{Var}(E[Y \mid X])$$

$$= E[4] + \text{Var}(0.9X)$$

$$= 4 + 0.81 \, \text{Var}(X)$$

$$= 4 + 0.81(2) = 4 + 1.62 = 5.62$$

$$\boxed{E[Y] = 9, \quad \text{Var}(Y) = 5.62}$$

---

### PROBLEM 3.4 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for Gaussian Mean

**Statement:** A robot's wheel odometry gives 5 independent distance measurements of a 1-meter reference: $\{0.98, 1.02, 0.99, 1.01, 1.00\}$ meters. Assuming i.i.d. Gaussian noise with known variance $\sigma^2 = 0.0004$, find the MLE estimate of the true distance $\mu$.

**Step 1: Write the likelihood function.**

For i.i.d. samples $x_1, \dots, x_n$ from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$:
$$L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2: Log-likelihood.**

$$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3: Differentiate and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\ell}{d\mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\mu = 0 \Rightarrow \mu_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 4: Plug in the values.**

$$\mu_{\text{MLE}} = \frac{0.98 + 1.02 + 0.99 + 1.01 + 1.00}{5} = \frac{5.00}{5} = 1.00$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{\text{MLE}} = 1.00 \text{ m}}$$

The MLE of the mean is the **sample mean** — a foundational result in statistical estimation theory.

---

### PROBLEM 3.5 — Sequential Bayesian Update (Robot Localization)

**Statement:** A robot is in one of 3 rooms, with prior beliefs $P(R_1)=0.5$, $P(R_2)=0.3$, $P(R_3)=0.2$. It then makes two independent sensor readings.
- Sensor 1 says "door" with likelihoods: $P(z_1 \mid R_1)=0.8$, $P(z_1 \mid R_2)=0.4$, $P(z_1 \mid R_3)=0.1$.
- Sensor 2 says "window" with likelihoods: $P(z_2 \mid R_1)=0.2$, $P(z_2 \mid R_2)=0.6$, $P(z_2 \mid R_3)=0.7$.

Find the posterior after both readings.

**Step 1: Update with $z_1$ (Bayes).**

For each $R_i$:
$$P(R_i \mid z_1) \propto P(z_1 \mid R_i) \, P(R_i)$$

Unnormalized:
- $R_1$: $0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.40$
- $R_2$: $0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12$
- $R_3$: $0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02$

Normalizer: $0.40 + 0.12 + 0.02 = 0.54$

After $z_1$:
- $P(R_1 \mid z_1) = 0.40/0.54 \approx 0.7407$
- $P(R_2 \mid z_1) = 0.12/0.54 \approx 0.2222$
- $P(R_3 \mid z_1) = 0.02/0.54 \approx 0.0370$

**Step 2: Update with $z_2$ using the new prior.**

$$P(R_i \mid z_1, z_2) \propto P(z_2 \mid R_i) \, P(R_i \mid z_1)$$

Unnormalized:
- $R_1$: $0.2 \times 0.7407 = 0.1481$
- $R_2$: $0.6 \times 0.2222 = 0.1333$
- $R_3$: $0.7 \times 0.0370 = 0.0259$

Normalizer: $0.1481 + 0.1333 + 0.0259 = 0.3074$

Final posterior:
- $P(R_1 \mid z_1, z_2) = 0.1481/0.3074 \approx 0.4819$
- $P(R_2 \mid z_1, z_2) = 0.1333/0.3074 \approx 0.4338$
- $P(R_3 \mid z_1, z_2) = 0.0259/0.3074 \approx 0.0843$

$$\boxed{P(R_1 \mid z_1,z_2) \approx 0.482, \quad P(R_2 \mid z_1,z_2) \approx 0.434, \quad P(R_3 \mid z_1,z_2) \approx 0.084}$$

Notice how the prior dominance of $R_1$ was *eroded* by contradictory sensor evidence — a beautiful demonstration of Bayesian learning in action.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams visualize the architecture of probabilistic reasoning and the Bayes filter — the central algorithmic structure of Module 1.

### 4.1 The Bayes Filter Loop (Robot Belief Update Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start New Time Step t] --> B[Receive Control u_t]
    B --> C[Predict Phase bel_bar x_t = integral p x_t vert x_t-1 u_t times bel x_t-1 dx_t-1]
    C --> D[Receive Measurement z_t]
    D --> E[Update Phase bel x_t = eta p z_t vert x_t times bel_bar x_t]
    E --> F{Belief Converged or t equals T}
    F -- No --> A
    F -- Yes --> G[Output Posterior State Estimate]
    G --> H[End]
```

### 4.2 Probability Problem-Solving Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Read the Problem Statement] --> Q{Is prior probability given?}
    Q -- Yes --> R{Is likelihood P of z given x given?}
    Q -- No --> S{Is independence stated?}
    R -- Yes --> T[Apply Bayes Theorem p x vert z = p z vert x p x divided by p z]
    R -- No --> U[Derive likelihood from sensor model Gaussian Poisson etc]
    S -- Yes --> V[Use Product Rule p A and B = p A times p B]
    S -- No --> W[Apply Total Probability p A = sum p A vert B_i times p B_i]
    T --> X[Normalize by marginal p z]
    U --> X
    V --> Y[Compute desired probability]
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z[Sanity Check 0 leq P leq 1]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Probabilistic Robotics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSING_LAYER [SENSING LAYER]
        S1[LiDAR Sensor]
        S2[Camera Sensor]
        S3[IMU Sensor]
        S4[Wheel Odometry]
    end
    subgraph NOISE_MODEL [NOISE MODELING]
        N1[Gaussian Additive Noise p z vert x]
        N2[Outlier Rejection 3 Sigma Rule]
    end
    subgraph BAYES_CORE [BAYESIAN INFERENCE CORE]
        B1[Prior Belief p x]
        B2[Bayes Update p x vert z proportional to p z vert x p x]
        B3[Posterior Belief bel x]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [STATE OUTPUT]
        O1[Mean Estimate mu]
        O2[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
        O3[Most Likely Pose arg max bel x]
    end
    S1 --> N1
    S2 --> N1
    S3 --> N1
    S4 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> O1
    B3 --> O2
    B3 --> O3
```

### 4.4 MLE vs MAP Estimation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Collect Data Samples x1 to xn] --> E{Is prior knowledge available?}
    E -- No --> F[Apply MLE theta_MLE = arg max L theta]
    E -- Yes --> G[Apply MAP theta_MAP = arg max L theta times p theta]
    F --> H[Result: Sample Mean and Sample Variance for Gaussian]
    G --> I[Result: Regularized Estimate incorporating Prior]
    H --> J[Use Estimate for Robot State Inference]
    I --> J
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q1.** State and explain **Bayes' Theorem** in the context of robot perception. Why is it the cornerstone of probabilistic robotics?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Bayes' Theorem states: $P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}$
- In robotics, with state $x$ and measurement $z$: $P(x \mid z) = \frac{P(z \mid x) \, P(x)}{P(z)}$
- $P(x)$ is the prior belief (before measurement), $P(z \mid x)$ is the sensor likelihood model, and $P(x \mid z)$ is the posterior (after measurement).
- It is the cornerstone because it provides the **only mathematically consistent method** to invert a generative sensor model — turning "what would the sensor read if I were at $x$?" into "what is my state, given what the sensor actually read?"
- Enables recursive belief updates in localization, mapping, and SLAM. **(1 mark for statement, 1 for notation, 1 for justification)**

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **expectation, variance, and covariance**. Give a one-line engineering use-case for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Expectation $E[X]$** — the long-run average value of a random variable. *Use-case:* mean position estimate of a robot.
- **Variance $\sigma^2$** — measure of spread around the mean. *Use-case:* uncertainty ellipse in robot localization.
- **Covariance $\text{Cov}(X,Y)$** — joint variability of two random variables. *Use-case:* coupling of $x$ and $y$ coordinates in a Kalman Filter state estimate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Bayes' Filter & Multivariate Gaussian**

**(a)** A robot operating in a 2D plane has a pose estimate represented by a bivariate Gaussian with mean $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$ and covariance $\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.16 \end{bmatrix}$. **(7 marks)**
    (i) Compute the correlation coefficient between $x$ and $y$.
    (ii) Compute the Mahalanobis distance from the point $\mathbf{x}_0 = (5.5, 3.4)^T$.
    (iii) Determine if this point is within the $1\sigma$ confidence ellipse (i.e., $d^2 \leq 2$ in 2D).

**(b)** Derive the **recursive Bayes filter** update equations, clearly distinguishing the **prediction step** and **measurement update step**. State two assumptions needed for the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Correlation coefficient:**
$\sigma_x = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5$, $\sigma_y = \sqrt{0.16} = 0.4$.
$\rho = \frac{\Sigma_{12}}{\sigma_x \sigma_y} = \frac{0.05}{(0.5)(0.4)} = \frac{0.05}{0.20} = 0.25$.
Hence, the coordinates are **weakly positively correlated**. **(2 marks)**

**(a) (ii) Mahalanobis distance:**
$\mathbf{x}_0 - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$
$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.0375}\begin{bmatrix} 0.16 & -0.05 \\ -0.05 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.267 & -1.333 \\ -1.333 & 6.667 \end{bmatrix}$
$d^2 = (0.5)(0.5 \times 4.267 - 0.4 \times 1.333) + (0.4)(0.5 \times (-1.333) + 0.4 \times 6.667)$
$= (0.5)(2.1333 - 0.5333) + (0.4)(-0.6667 + 2.6667)$
$= (0.5)(1.6) + (0.4)(2.0) = 0.8 + 0.8 = 1.6$. **(3 marks)**

**(a) (iii) Confidence check:**
Since $d^2 = 1.6 < 2$, the point is **inside the $1\sigma$ confidence ellipse**. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Bayes Filter Derivation:**
Starting from the recursive posterior:
$$P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \, P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$
where $\eta$ is a normalizer.
Expanding the second term using the law of total probability over $x_{t-1}$:
$$P(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Combining:
$$\boxed{\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. **Markov property:** The state $x_t$ is complete, so $P(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$.
2. **Conditional independence of measurements:** $P(z_t \mid x_{0:t}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = P(z_t \mid x_t)$.

The first factor in the integral is the **prediction step** (motion model), and the outer $P(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot (\cdot)$ is the **measurement update step**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — MLE, MAP, and Statistical Estimation**

**(a)** Define **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** and **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimation. For a Gaussian likelihood with known variance and a Gaussian prior, derive the MAP estimate of the mean and explain how it differs from the MLE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot takes $n=10$ independent range measurements to a wall: $\{2.01, 1.99, 2.02, 1.98, 2.00, 2.01, 1.99, 2.02, 2.00, 1.98\}$ m. Assuming additive Gaussian noise with $\sigma^2 = 0.0004$ and a Gaussian prior on the true distance with $\mu_0 = 1.95$ and $\sigma_0^2 = 0.01$, compute **(i)** the MLE estimate, **(ii)** the MAP estimate, and **(iii)** the variance of the MAP estimate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MLE vs MAP:**
- **MLE:** $\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_{\theta} L(\theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \mid \theta)$
- **MAP:** $\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\theta \mid D) = \arg\max_{\theta} P(D \mid \theta) P(\theta)$

For Gaussian likelihood $P(x_i \mid \mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x_i-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ and Gaussian prior $P(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma_0\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(\mu-\mu_0)^2/(2\sigma_0^2)}$:
$$\mu_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\mu}\left[-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i-\mu)^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma_0^2}(\mu-\mu_0)^2\right]$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:
$$\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu) - \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2}(\mu - \mu_0) = 0$$

$$\mu_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{\sigma_0^2 \sum x_i + \sigma^2 n \mu_0}{n \sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2} = \frac{\sigma_0^2 \, n \, \bar{x} + \sigma^2 \, \mu_0}{n\sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2}$$

This is a **convex combination** of the sample mean and the prior mean. MLE is a special case of MAP with $\sigma_0^2 \to \infty$ (uninformative prior). **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**(b)(i) MLE:**
$\bar{x} = \frac{2.01+1.99+2.02+1.98+2.00+2.01+1.99+2.02+2.00+1.98}{10} = \frac{20.00}{10} = 2.00$
$\mu_{\text{MLE}} = 2.00$ m. **(2 marks)**

**(b)(ii) MAP:**
$\mu_{\text{MAP}} = \frac{(0.01)(10)(2.00) + (0.0004)(1.95)}{(10)(0.01) + 0.0004} = \frac{0.20 + 0.00078}{0.1004} = \frac{0.20078}{0.1004} \approx 2.00$ m. **(3 marks)**

**(b)(iii) Variance of MAP:**
For Gaussian-Gaussian conjugate model, posterior precision = sum of precisions:
$\frac{1}{\sigma_{\text{MAP}}^2} = \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} = \frac{10}{0.0004} + \frac{1}{0.01} = 25000 + 100 = 25100$
$\sigma_{\text{MAP}}^2 = \frac{1}{25100} \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-5}$ m². **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalizer in Bayes' Theorem** — always compute the total probability $P(z)$ and divide. Skipping this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Sign errors in Mahalanobis distance** — the quadratic form $(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ must be a *scalar*. Re-check matrix dimensions.
> 3. **Confusing MLE and MAP** — MLE uses only likelihood; MAP uses likelihood × prior. State the prior explicitly or be marked down.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — distances in **meters**, angles in **radians**, probabilities **dimensionless**.
> 5. **Violating $\sum P(x_i) = 1$** — discrete probabilities must sum to 1; always normalize.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** (Markov, conditional independence) in Bayes filter derivations — typically costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Problem Sets on Probability & Statistics**
> - [ ] Know the **three Kolmogorov axioms** (non-negativity, normalization, additivity).
> - [ ] Master **conditional probability** and the **product rule**.
> - [ ] Be fluent in **Bayes' Theorem** — both the formula and its robotics interpretation.
> - [ ] Be able to apply the **Law of Total Probability** for normalizing constants.
> - [ ] Compute **expectation, variance, covariance, correlation** from data and from distributions.
> - [ ] Write the **1D and multivariate Gaussian PDF** from memory; compute the Mahalanobis distance.
> - [ ] Know the **68-95-99.7 rule** for confidence intervals.
> - [ ] Understand **independence vs conditional independence** (key for SLAM factor graphs).
> - [ ] Derive and explain the **recursive Bayes filter** (predict + update).
> - [ ] Differentiate **MLE vs MAP**; derive Gaussian-Gaussian conjugate update.
> - [ ] Apply the **Law of Total Expectation and Variance** for hierarchical estimation.
> - [ ] Always **normalize discrete probabilities** and check the range $0 \le P \le 1$.
> - [ ] State the **Markov property** and **measurement conditional independence** assumptions.
> - [ ] Recognize the **base-rate fallacy** — high sensor accuracy ≠ high posterior certainty when prior is low.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT863 - PROBABILISTIC ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Readings and Essays on the Significance of Probabilistic Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probabilistic Robotics** is a sub-field of robotics that uses statistical techniques, principally **Bayesian inference**, to represent the robot's belief about its state and the environment as **probability distributions** rather than single deterministic estimates. It addresses the fundamental problem that real-world robots face *uncertainty* in their sensors, actuators, and environment models.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is the foundational reading/essay module that justifies *why* an entire course is dedicated to probabilistic methods. The readings and seminal essays in this area (notably those compiled in **"Probabilistic Robotics" by Thrun, Burgard, and Fox, MIT Press, 2005**) argue that classical, deterministic AI/robotics pipelines fail catastrophically in the face of:
- **Sensor noise** (every measurement is corrupted).
- **Actuator stochasticity** (motors slip, wheels drift).
- **Partial observability** (the robot never sees the full state).
- **Dynamic environments** (people, other agents, weather).

The **significance** lies in replacing brittle point-estimate logic with mathematically principled reasoning under uncertainty.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Blindfolded Treasure Hunter Analogy**
>
> Imagine a person blindfolded inside a large house, asked to find a hidden key. They can only:
> - Feel the floor with their left foot (a noisy sensor).
> - Take steps that sometimes overshoot (noisy actuators).
> - Occasionally bump into walls they cannot see (partial observations).
>
> A **non-probabilistic** (deterministic) approach would say: *"The key is at coordinate (3, 5)."* If that guess is wrong by even a little, the entire mission fails.
>
> A **probabilistic** approach would say: *"I am **80% sure** the key is near (3, 5), **15% sure** it is at (2, 4), and **5% sure** it is somewhere else."* As new foot-feelings and bumps occur, these percentages **update** mathematically. The robot does not "know" where the key is — it maintains a *belief distribution* and acts optimally on it.

## 1.3 Core Quantities and Constants Used in the Field

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $p(x)$ | Probability of state $x$ | Unitless, $0 \le p \le 1$ |
| $p(x \vert y)$ | Conditional probability of $x$ given $y$ | Unitless |
| $\mu$ | Mean of belief distribution | Meters / Radians |
| $\sigma^2$ | Variance (uncertainty) | $\text{m}^2$ or $\text{rad}^2$ |
| $\Sigma$ | Covariance matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| $N(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Multivariate Gaussian | Standard form |
| $\eta$ | Normalizer (Bayes' rule) | Scalar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fundamental *currency* of probabilistic robotics is the **belief**:
>
> $$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$
>
> where $x_t$ is the state at time $t$, $z_{1:t}$ are measurements up to time $t$, and $u_{1:t}$ are control inputs. This single equation is the *philosophical backbone* of the entire field.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

- It sets the **philosophical motivation** for every algorithm studied later in the course (Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, SLAM, etc.).
- The essays/readings establish the **vocabulary** (belief, posterior, likelihood, prior, evidence) that the rest of the syllabus depends upon.
- It directly addresses **CO1** of typical KTU PERAT863 outcomes: *"Understand the need for probabilistic techniques in robotics and articulate the limitations of deterministic approaches."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Significance (Essay-Level Argumentation)

The seminal essays and readings on probabilistic robotics converge on **three pillars** that justify the field. Examiners frequently test a student's ability to articulate these clearly.

### Pillar 1 — *Inherent Uncertainty is Unavoidable*

- **Sensor noise:** Even high-grade LiDARs have centimeter-level Gaussian-distributed errors. Camera pixels suffer from photon shot noise, motion blur, and exposure variation.
- **Actuator noise:** Wheel encoders integrate slip; quadrotor thrust is affected by wind gusts; robotic arms have backlash and joint compliance.
- **Model uncertainty:** Maps are approximations. Friction coefficients are estimates. Even the kinematic model is an idealization.
- **Why it matters:** A deterministic planner that assumes perfect information will fail the moment reality deviates from the model. Probabilistic methods *expect* noise and reason optimally over it.

### Pillar 2 — *The Mathematical Tool Already Exists: Probability Theory*

- Bayes' rule, the Law of Total Probability, and Markov Chain theory are **mature, well-understood** mathematical frameworks.
- They give a *principled*, *optimal* way to fuse information from multiple noisy sources.
- The **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** proves that no estimator can do better than a certain accuracy limit — probability theory lets us *approach* that limit.

### Pillar 3 — *It Scales and Composes*

- Probabilistic algorithms are **modular**: a localization module's output (a belief distribution) can be fed directly into a planning module.
- Algorithms like the **Bayes Filter** are *recursive* — only the current belief and the new measurement/control are needed, not the entire history.
- The same framework unifies disparate problems: localization, mapping, SLAM, tracking, and decision-making all become special cases of the same recursion.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **core mathematical vocabulary** of the significance essay. Memorize the meaning of each; examiners often award partial marks for correctly naming what each symbol represents.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Rule | $p(x \vert z) = \eta \, p(z \vert x) \, p(x)$ | Posterior ∝ Likelihood × Prior |
| 2 | Total Probability | $p(z) = \sum_{x} p(z \vert x) \, p(x)$ | Marginal likelihood (evidence) |
| 3 | Bayes Filter Recursion | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Two-step: predict, then update |
| 4 | Markov Assumption | $p(x_t \vert x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | State is sufficient statistic |
| 5 | Sensor Model | $p(z_t \vert x_t, m)$ | Likelihood of measurement given state & map |
| 6 | Motion Model | $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$ | Probabilistic state transition |
| 7 | Gaussian PDF | $p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Most common noise model |
| 8 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_M = \sqrt{(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)}$ | Statistically scaled distance |
| 9 | Expected Value | $\mathbb{E}[x] = \int x \, p(x) \, dx$ | Optimal Bayesian estimate under MSE |
| 10 | Covariance | $\Sigma = \mathbb{E}[(x-\mu)(x-\mu)^T]$ | Uncertainty of multivariate belief |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination tip (KTU 2024):** In 14-mark essay questions on "significance of probabilistic robotics," at least **2 marks** are reserved for correctly identifying and naming the relevant probability law. Misnaming Bayes' rule as "the Bayes theorem" without the `posterior ∝ prior × likelihood` structure costs marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Probabilistic Technique Used |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., Waymo, Tesla FSD) | Self-driving cars in traffic | Particle-filter localization, Kalman SLAM |
| Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva) | Goods-to-person fulfillment | Monte Carlo Localization on a known map |
| Aerial Drones (DJI, Skydio) | GPS-denied navigation | Visual-inertial SLAM with EKF |
| Medical Robotics (Da Vinci) | Surgical instrument tracking | Probabilistic registration |
| Planetary Rovers (NASA Perseverance) | Visual Terrain Relative Navigation | Bayesian terrain classification |
| Underwater AUVs (REMUS) | Subsea pipeline inspection | Particle filters with multi-beam sonar |
| Human-Robot Interaction | Tracking people in a room | Probabilistic multi-hypothesis tracking |

## 2.4 The "Significance" — Essay-Ready Argumentative Flow

A model 14-mark answer often follows this exact 4-beat structure:

1. **Beat 1 — Problem:** Real robots are uncertain. Deterministic methods break.
2. **Beat 2 — Solution:** Probability theory provides a principled, optimal framework.
3. **Beat 3 — Mechanism:** The Bayes Filter is a universal recursion that fuses sensor data and control inputs into a belief.
4. **Beat 4 — Impact:** Modern robotics (autonomous cars, drones, Mars rovers) would be impossible without probabilistic methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The strongest essays *contrast* deterministic and probabilistic approaches with a concrete example. For instance: *"A deterministic A* planner assumes the robot is exactly at the odometry-reported cell. In a slipping wheel scenario, this leads to a 2 m error that compounds. A probabilistic planner instead reasons over a Gaussian belief, planning to a region where the *expected* cost is minimized, gracefully handling the slip."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Even though "Significance" is a *conceptual* topic, the KTU 2024 scheme demands that students be able to **derive** the Bayes Filter recursion from first principles, because this is the mathematical embodiment of the significance argument. Below is the exhaustive derivation, step-by-step, with every algebraic transition explained.

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Filter Recursion from First Principles

### Goal

We want to compute the posterior belief over the current state $x_t$ given the *entire history* of measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

### Step 1 — Factor the History Using the Chain Rule of Probability

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Explanation:** The chain rule says $p(A, B \mid C) = p(A \mid B, C)\, p(B \mid C)$. We let $A = z_t$, $B = x_t$, $C = (z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ and apply the rule.

### Step 2 — Apply the Markov Assumption (Independence from Past Given Current State)

The Markov property states that the current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state $x_t$ (assuming a static environment, or that the environment's effect is captured in $x_t$):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

Similarly, the current state $x_t$ depends on the immediately previous state $x_{t-1}$ and the current control $u_t$, but not on earlier history:

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for the "significance" essay:** The Markov assumption is what makes the recursion *tractable*. Without it, the belief would depend on the entire infinite history, which is computationally impossible. This is the *mathematical reason* probability theory is so powerful for robotics — it compresses all relevant past information into a single state.

### Step 3 — Substitute Back

The numerator becomes:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$

The denominator, $p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$, is independent of $x_t$ and acts purely as a normalizer. We denote it as $\eta^{-1}$:

$$\eta^{-1} = p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

So:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$

### Step 4 — Marginalize over the Previous State

The transition term $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ still depends on the unknown previous state $x_{t-1}$. We eliminate it using the **Law of Total Probability**, integrating (or summing, for discrete state spaces) over all possible $x_{t-1}$:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

This is the **prediction step** — it propagates the previous belief through the motion model.

### Step 5 — Combine

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

This is the **measurement update step** — it multiplies the predicted belief by the likelihood of the new measurement.

### Final Recursion (Telescoping into 2 Steps)

Putting it all together:

$$\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t) \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This single boxed equation is the mathematical proof of the "significance" of probabilistic robotics.** It tells us: *if* we can specify a sensor model $p(z_t \vert x_t)$ and a motion model $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$, *then* we can in principle compute the optimal belief about the robot's state from any stream of sensor data and control inputs — no matter how noisy. That is the *power* the essays celebrate.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Belief Update (1-D Toy)

Suppose a robot's position is uncertain and we want to compute the belief at time $t=1$ given a uniform prior and a single noisy measurement.

**Setup:**
- State space: 3 cells, $x \in \{A, B, C\}$.
- Prior belief: $bel(x_0) = [0.2,\ 0.5,\ 0.3]$.
- Motion model: $p(x_1 = B \mid u_1, x_0 = A) = 0.7$, $p(x_1 = A \mid u_1, x_0 = A) = 0.2$, $p(x_1 = C \mid u_1, x_0 = A) = 0.1$ (and analogous for $B$, $C$).
- Sensor model: robot sees $z_1 = B$; likelihoods $p(z_1 = B \mid x_1) = [0.1,\ 0.8,\ 0.1]$.

**Step 1 — Prediction:**

For each possible current state $x_1$:

$$\overline{bel}(x_1 = A) = 0.2 \cdot 0.2 + 0.5 \cdot 0.1 + 0.3 \cdot 0.1 = 0.04 + 0.05 + 0.03 = 0.12$$

$$\overline{bel}(x_1 = B) = 0.2 \cdot 0.7 + 0.5 \cdot 0.7 + 0.3 \cdot 0.1 = 0.14 + 0.35 + 0.03 = 0.52$$

$$\overline{bel}(x_1 = C) = 0.2 \cdot 0.1 + 0.5 \cdot 0.2 + 0.3 \cdot 0.8 = 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.24 = 0.36$$

**Step 2 — Likelihood multiplication:**

$$bel(x_1 = A) \propto 0.12 \times 0.1 = 0.012$$

$$bel(x_1 = B) \propto 0.52 \times 0.8 = 0.416$$

$$bel(x_1 = C) \propto 0.36 \times 0.1 = 0.036$$

**Step 3 — Normalize ($\eta$):**

Sum $= 0.012 + 0.416 + 0.036 = 0.464$.

$$bel(x_1) = [0.012/0.464,\ 0.416/0.464,\ 0.036/0.464] = [0.0259,\ 0.8966,\ 0.0776]$$

**Result:** Belief on cell $B$ jumped from 50% to **89.66%** — a clean demonstration of the *significance* of probabilistic update: a single noisy measurement dramatically sharpens the belief.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the Bayes Filter to Real Algorithms

| Bayes Filter Component | Closed-Form Specialization | Resulting Algorithm | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $bel(x_t)$ as Gaussian | Linear $p(z_t \vert x_t)$, linear $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$ | **Kalman Filter (KF)** | Indoor wheeled robot, linear systems |
| $bel(x_t)$ as Gaussian | Nonlinear but local-linearizable | **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** | SLAM, INS/GPS fusion |
| $bel(x_t)$ as set of particles | Non-Gaussian, multimodal | **Particle Filter (MCL)** | Global localization, kidnapped robot |
| $bel(x_t)$ as grid | Discrete finite environment | **Histogram Filter** | Small occupancy grid maps |
| $bel(x_t)$, $m$ as Gaussians on landmarks | EKF over landmarks | **EKF-SLAM** | Early SLAM systems |
| $bel(x_t)$, $m$ as graph of constraints | Sparse nonlinear least squares | **Graph-SLAM** | Modern large-scale SLAM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The significance essay's punchline:** Every probabilistic robotics algorithm studied in the rest of this KTU course is a *special case* of the Bayes Filter. This unification is the central intellectual contribution of the field, and examiners expect you to recognize it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture: The Probabilistic Robotics Pipeline

This diagram shows how the philosophical ideas of probabilistic robotics translate into a working software architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real World Environment] --> B[Noisy Sensors]
    A --> C[Noisy Actuators]
    B --> D[Measurement z_t]
    C --> E[Control u_t]
    D --> F[Bayes Filter Engine]
    E --> F
    G[Previous Belief bel x t-1] --> F
    F --> H[Predicted Belief bar bel x t]
    H --> I[Update via Likelihood p zt xt]
    I --> J[Posterior Belief bel x t]
    J --> K[Decision / Planner]
    K --> L[Action Selection]
    L --> C
    J --> M[Localization Module]
    J --> N[Mapping Module]
    J --> O[SLAM Module]
    M --> P[Robot State Estimate]
    N --> Q[Environment Map]
    O --> R[Simultaneous State and Map]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **outer feedback loop** (World → Sensors → Bayes Filter → Planner → Actuators → World) is the *sense–think–act* cycle of any intelligent robot.
- The **inner loop** (Previous Belief → Prediction → Update → Posterior Belief) is the *recursive belief update*, executed at every time step.
- The **three output modules** (Localization, Mapping, SLAM) are the *three classical task families* of probabilistic robotics.

## 4.2 Conceptual Map of the "Significance" Argument

This diagram captures the logical flow of the *essay* itself — useful when planning a 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Real robots are uncertain] --> S2[Deterministic methods fail]
    S2 --> S3[Probability theory is mature]
    S3 --> S4[Bayes Filter is a universal recursion]
    S4 --> S5[Specialize to KF, EKF, PF, SLAM]
    S5 --> S6[Modern robotics depends on this]
    S1 --> P1[Sensor noise]
    S1 --> P2[Actuator noise]
    S1 --> P3[Model error]
    S1 --> P4[Partial observability]
    P1 --> S2
    P2 --> S2
    P3 --> S2
    P4 --> S2
    S6 --> A1[Autonomous cars]
    S6 --> A2[Drones]
    S6 --> A3[Mars rovers]
    S6 --> A4[Medical robots]
```

**Reading guide:** The left column lists the **causes of uncertainty**, the middle column lists the **logical chain of the essay**, and the right column lists the **engineering applications**. A complete KTU answer should touch all three columns.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Bayes Filter as a Signal Processing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[bel x t-1] --> MUL1[Multiply by motion model]
    INT[Integrate over x t-1] --> MUL1
    MUL1 --> PRED[Predicted belief bar bel x t]
    PRED --> MUL2[Multiply by likelihood p zt xt]
    MEAS[Measurement z t] --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> NORM[Normalize by eta]
    NORM --> OUT1[bel x t]
    OUT1 --> DLY[Delay one time step]
    DLY --> IN1
    OUT1 --> USE[Use for planning or estimation]
```

**Key takeaway:** Notice the **delay block** — this is what makes the filter *recursive*. The same belief is delayed and fed back as input for the next time step. This is mathematically identical to a digital IIR filter in classical signal processing; the Bayes Filter is the **nonlinear, probabilistic generalization** of an IIR filter.

## 4.4 Comparison: Deterministic vs Probabilistic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DETERM[Deterministic Pipeline]
        D1[Sensor Reading] --> D2[Point Estimate of State]
        D2 --> D3[Deterministic Planner]
        D3 --> D4[Open-loop or simple feedback]
    end
    subgraph PROB[Probabilistic Pipeline]
        P1[Sensor Reading] --> P2[Belief Distribution p x t]
        P2 --> P3[Bayesian Update]
        P3 --> P4[Expected cost or risk minimization]
        P4 --> P5[Probabilistic Planner]
    end
    D4 --> FRAG[Brittle to noise and model error]
    P5 --> ROB[Robust to noise and model error]
```

**Reading guide:** The contrast in the bottom row (`FRAG` vs `ROB`) is the **single most important sentence** in any 14-mark significance essay.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT863, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q1. Define "probabilistic robotics" and state any two sources of uncertainty in robot perception.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Probabilistic robotics is the field of robotics that employs statistical methods — primarily Bayesian inference — to represent a robot's knowledge of its own state and the surrounding environment as *probability distributions* rather than as single deterministic values. This allows the robot to reason optimally under uncertainty.
- **Two sources of uncertainty (1 mark, ½ each):**
  1. **Sensor noise** — every real measurement is corrupted; e.g., LiDAR returns have Gaussian-distributed range errors.
  2. **Actuator noise** — commanded motions are not perfectly executed; e.g., wheel slip in mobile robots.

> *(Acceptable alternative for second source: model/environment uncertainty, partial observability, dynamic environments.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT863, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Q2. What is meant by a "belief" in probabilistic robotics? Write the formal expression for belief at time $t$.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
- A **belief** is the robot's *internal probabilistic estimate* of its own state, given the entire history of sensor measurements and control actions it has experienced. It is *not* a single number but a probability distribution over all possible states. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Formal expression:**

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where $x_t$ = state at time $t$, $z_{1:t}$ = measurements from time $1$ to $t$, $u_{1:t}$ = control inputs from time $1$ to $t$. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer / Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - Essays that *only* define Bayes' rule without linking it to a robot example lose 4–5 marks.
> - Students often confuse $p(x \vert z)$ with $p(z \vert x)$. Misnaming the *likelihood* as the *posterior* is a recurring 2-mark penalty.
> - Vague phrases like "probability is used because of noise" without naming the *specific* noise model (Gaussian, uniform, multimodal) cost 1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> - Failing to **specialize** the Bayes Filter into KF / EKF / PF is treated as a missing key concept (−2 marks).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT863, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Essay on Significance + Bayes Filter Derivation**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*"Discuss, with examples, the significance of probabilistic methods in modern robotics. Why are deterministic approaches inadequate?"*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify sources of uncertainty (2 marks):**
- Sensor noise (LiDAR, camera, IMU all produce corrupted data).
- Actuator noise (wheel slip, backlash, control delay).
- Model/environment uncertainty (imperfect maps, unknown friction, dynamic obstacles).
- Partial observability (the robot never directly observes its full state).

**Step 2 — Show how deterministic methods fail (2 marks):**
- Deterministic planners (e.g., classical A* on an occupancy grid) commit to a *single* state estimate.
- A small slip or sensor glitch causes a large *catastrophic* deviation.
- Example: A deterministic vacuum-cleaning robot estimates it is at cell (5,5); if wheel slip places it at (5,6) without correction, the entire coverage plan becomes invalid.

**Step 3 — Argue the probabilistic solution (2 marks):**
- Probability theory provides the *mathematically principled* way to fuse noisy information.
- Bayes' rule gives the *optimal* way to update beliefs given new evidence.
- The Bayes Filter is a *universal recursion* that subsumes localization, mapping, and SLAM as special cases.

**Step 4 — Conclude with impact (1 mark):**
- Modern systems (Waymo, Mars Perseverance, DJI drones) are all *fundamentally probabilistic*. Without these methods, the field would be stuck in lab environments.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*"Derive the Bayes Filter recursion from the definition of conditional probability. Clearly state any assumptions made."*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the belief (1 mark):**

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 — Apply chain rule (2 marks):**

$$bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Markov assumption (2 marks):**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$

**Step 4 — Marginalize and introduce normalizer (2 marks):**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Final boxed answer (above equation, 0 marks — already in Step 4):**
*(Note: The derivation is complete; the boxed result is the conclusion of Step 4.)*

**Assumption to be stated explicitly:** *Markov assumption — the current state $x_t$ is a sufficient statistic for the past, i.e., the future is conditionally independent of the past given the present.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERAT863, 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternate Essay: Probabilistic vs Deterministic + Specializations**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*"Compare and contrast deterministic and probabilistic approaches to robot localization. Illustrate with a worked numerical example showing how a single noisy measurement updates a belief."*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define deterministic localization (1 mark):**
- The robot maintains a *single* state estimate, e.g., $(x, y, \theta) = (3.20, 1.50, 0.78)$. No uncertainty is represented.

**Step 2 — Define probabilistic localization (1 mark):**
- The robot maintains a *belief distribution* $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, typically a Gaussian or a set of particles.

**Step 3 — Compare strengths/weaknesses (1 mark):**
- Deterministic: simple, fast, but brittle. Probabilistic: principled, robust, but computationally heavier.

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example (4 marks):**
*(Use the same example as in Section 3.2 of these notes.)*
- Prior belief: $bel(x_0) = [0.2, 0.5, 0.3]$.
- Motion model given.
- Likelihood of measurement $z_1 = B$: $p(z_1 = B \mid x_1) = [0.1, 0.8, 0.1]$.
- Compute predicted belief: $[0.12, 0.52, 0.36]$.
- Multiply by likelihood: $[0.012, 0.416, 0.036]$.
- Normalize by $\eta = 1/0.464 \approx 2.155$.
- Final belief: $[0.026, 0.897, 0.078]$.
- **Interpretation:** Belief at $B$ jumps from 50% to 89.7% — a *quantitative* demonstration of probabilistic significance.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*"Explain how the Bayes Filter is specialized into different algorithms. Describe any two such specializations, the assumptions they make, and the kinds of problems they solve."*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — General Bayes Filter statement (1 mark):**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 2 — Specialization 1: Kalman Filter (3 marks):**
- **Assumption:** Beliefs are *unimodal Gaussian*, sensor and motion models are *linear*.
- **Consequence:** The integral becomes a closed-form linear update; the posterior is exactly Gaussian.
- **Use case:** Indoor wheeled robots with linear odometry and range sensors; aerospace INS/GPS fusion.
- **Equation:**

$$x_t = A x_{t-1} + B u_t + w_t,\ \ w_t \sim N(0, Q)$$
$$z_t = H x_t + v_t,\ \ v_t \sim N(0, R)$$

**Step 3 — Specialization 2: Particle Filter (Monte Carlo Localization) (3 marks):**
- **Assumption:** Belief is represented by a *finite set of weighted samples* (particles); no functional form assumed.
- **Consequence:** Can represent *multimodal* and *non-Gaussian* beliefs; the integral becomes a Monte Carlo sum.
- **Use case:** *Global* localization (kidnapped robot problem), highly nonlinear sensor models (e.g., beam-based LiDAR with occlusions).
- **Resampling step:** Particles with low importance weight are replaced by copies of high-weight particles.

**Step 4 — Concluding sentence (0 marks — built into Step 2/3):** The unification under the Bayes Filter is the *central intellectual contribution* of probabilistic robotics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU exam prep):**

- ☐ Probabilistic robotics = statistics + robotics to handle *uncertainty*.
- ☐ Four sources of uncertainty: **sensor noise, actuator noise, model error, partial observability**.
- ☐ Belief is a *distribution*, not a point: $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- ☐ Bayes' rule structure: **posterior = η × likelihood × prior**.
- ☐ Bayes Filter has *two* steps: **prediction** (motion model) and **update** (sensor model).
- ☐ **Markov assumption** is the key simplification that makes the recursion tractable.
- ☐ Three classical probabilistic algorithms to remember: **KF (linear-Gaussian), EKF (local-linear Gaussian), PF / MCL (non-parametric, multimodal)**.
- ☐ Modern applications to *name* in essays: autonomous cars, Mars rovers, drones, warehouse robots, medical robots.
- ☐ The Bayes Filter is the **mathematical embodiment** of the significance argument.
- ☐ The contrast: deterministic = *brittle*; probabilistic = *robust*. Always end an essay with this contrast.
- ☐ For numerical belief-update problems, always show **three steps**: predict, multiply, normalize.
- ☐ Normalizer $\eta$ is *not* optional — omitting it loses marks.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PERAT863, MODULE 1, TOPIC: READINGS AND ESSAYS ON THE SIGNIFICANCE OF PROBABILISTIC ROBOTICS**
