# 📘 Module 3 — Case Study Analyses and Practical Demonstrations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📖 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Case Study Analyses and Practical Demonstrations** in Probabilistic Robotics refers to the systematic examination, decomposition, and performance evaluation of real-world robotic systems whose behaviour is governed by stochastic models. In the KTU 2024 scheme, a "case study" is treated as a structured engineering investigation that requires a student to:

1. Identify the underlying probability distributions (motion noise $w_t$, sensor noise $v_t$).
2. Apply a Bayesian inference framework (prediction–update cycle).
3. Quantify algorithmic performance against standardised metrics such as **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)**, **Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE)**, and **Relative Pose Error (RPE)**.
4. Critically interpret results using **Cramér–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** or **Mean Effective Sample Size (N\_eff)**.

Mathematically, every practical demonstration reduces to evaluating the posterior:

$$
bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
$$

where the *case study* asks: *"How does the choice of proposal distribution or sensor model affect the accuracy and convergence of this posterior?"*

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Navigator in a Foggy Warehouse"

Imagine a warehouse worker (the robot) is blindfolded inside a vast, foggy warehouse (the unknown environment). Every step they take is uncertain (motion noise), and every echo they hear from a wall (ultrasonic/sonar reading) is corrupted (sensor noise). They cannot *know* their position, but they can keep a **belief** — a probability cloud — over where they might be.

- A **case study** is the worker's *logbook*: it records which probabilistic algorithm they used, what evidence they gathered, and how their belief cloud shrank over time.
- A **practical demonstration** is the *walk-through*: replaying the logbook on a real dataset (e.g., the **Victoria Park Dataset** or **KITTI Benchmark**) to measure performance.
- The "fog" is quantified by the variance $\sigma^2$ of the process noise; the "echo error" is the variance of measurement noise.

Just as a navigator with a good compass (high-precision sensor) and a reliable stride (low-noise odometry) converges to the true aisle faster, a robot whose probabilistic model matches reality has a sharper, more accurate belief distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Case study questions almost always test *interpretation* rather than mere formula substitution. You must connect every numerical result back to a physical consequence (e.g., "lower $\sigma_{odometry}$ implies faster convergence but higher sensitivity to wheel slip").

### 📏 Standard Performance Metrics Used in Case Studies

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Use-Case |
|--------|--------|------------------|
| Root Mean Square Error | RMSE | Pose estimation accuracy |
| Absolute Trajectory Error | ATE | SLAM global consistency |
| Relative Pose Error | RPE | Drift over short intervals |
| Effective Sample Size | $N_{\text{eff}}$ | Particle filter degeneracy check |
| Normalised Estimation Error Squared | NEES | Filter consistency (chi-squared test) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 Analytical Framework for a Probabilistic Robotics Case Study

A KTU-2024 board-style case study follows a **5-stage analytical pipeline**. Each stage maps to a specific Bayesian concept taught in Module 3.

### 🔹 Stage 1 — Problem Decomposition

Break the case into its two probability streams:

$$
\underbrace{p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)}_{\text{Motion Model}} \quad , \quad \underbrace{p(z_t \mid x_t)}_{\text{Sensor Model}}
$$

- Identify whether the case is a **localization**, **mapping**, or **SLAM** problem.
- Determine the state vector $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^\top$ (or $[x, y, z, \text{roll}, \text{pitch}, \text{yaw}]^\top$ for 6-DoF).

### 🔹 Stage 2 — Uncertainty Characterisation

For every noise term, compute or read off the **standard deviation**:

$$
\text{Motion noise: } \sigma_u^2 \quad , \quad \text{Sensor noise: } \sigma_z^2
$$

For multivariate noise, use the **covariance matrix**:

$$
\mathbf{Q}_{t} = E[w_t \, w_t^\top] \quad , \quad \mathbf{R}_{t} = E[v_t \, v_t^\top]
$$

### 🔹 Stage 3 — Algorithm Selection

Choose the appropriate Bayes filter:

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Algorithm |
|------------------------|----------------------|
| Linear Gaussian + unimodal | Kalman Filter (KF) |
| Mild non-linearity | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) |
| Strong non-linearity | Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) |
| Multi-modal belief | Particle Filter / Monte Carlo Localization |
| Unknown map + unknown pose | FastSLAM / Graph-SLAM |

### 🔹 Stage 4 — Posterior Evaluation

Use **Monte Carlo** or **analytic** methods to evaluate the posterior. For EKF:

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1}) \quad ; \quad \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + Q_t
$$

$$
K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)) \quad ; \quad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

### 🔹 Stage 5 — Performance Validation

Apply the **chi-squared consistency test** (NEES):

$$
\epsilon_t = (x_t - \hat{x}_t)^\top \Sigma_t^{-1} (x_t - \hat{x}_t)
$$

If averaged over $N$ runs, the test statistic should fall within the **95% confidence bounds** $\chi^2_{r,0.025} \le \bar{\epsilon}_t \le \chi^2_{r,0.975}$, where $r$ is the state dimension.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Never write absolute value bars `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table cell** — it breaks the parser. The following table uses LaTeX-rendered expressions safely.

| # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Bayes Filter (recursive) | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t) \int p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Foundation of every case study |
| 2 | EKF Prediction | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1}) \, ; \, \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + Q_t$ | Wheel-odometry + gyroscope fusion |
| 3 | EKF Update (Kalman Gain) | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + R_t)^{-1}$ | Lidar / camera correction step |
| 4 | Particle Filter Resampling | Resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$, where $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{(i)})^2$ | Prevent particle degeneracy |
| 5 | Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) | Sample $x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)$, weight $w_t^{(i)} = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t^{(i)})$ | Global localization in known map |
| 6 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert x_i - \hat{x}_i \rVert^2}$ | SLAM trajectory accuracy |
| 7 | NEES Consistency | $\bar{\epsilon}_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} (x_t^{(j)} - \hat{x}_t^{(j)})^\top (\Sigma_t^{(j)})^{-1} (x_t^{(j)} - \hat{x}_t^{(j)})$ | Filter over/under-confidence check |
| 8 | Beam Range Finder Model | $p(z_t \vert x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} p_{\text{hit}} \, p_{\text{short}} \, p_{\text{max}} \, p_{\text{rand}}$ | Likelihood field / ray-casting |
| 9 | Cramér–Rao Lower Bound | $\text{Var}(\hat{x}) \ge \mathcal{I}^{-1}(\theta)$ (Fisher Information) | Theoretical minimum error |
| 10 | Odometry Motion Model | $x_t = x_{t-1} \oplus \begin{bmatrix} \hat{x}_{t-1} \\ \hat{y}_{t-1} \\ \hat{\theta}_{t-1} \end{bmatrix} + w_t$ | Diff-drive / Ackermann steering |

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Case studies compare EKF vs. UKF for GNSS-IMU fusion at 100 Hz.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, MiR):** MCL with **likelihood-field** sensor model on 2-D lidar.
- **Vacuum Robots (iRobot Roomba):** Particle filter with low-cost wheel odometry ($\sigma_v \approx 0.1$ m, $\sigma_\gamma \approx 5^\circ$).
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Visual SLAM with UKF-based Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO).
- **Drones (DJI, Skydio):** Graph-SLAM with loop closure using ORB-SLAM3 features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board examiners expect students to quote **at least one concrete metric value** when discussing a case study. Memorise the typical RMSE ranges: indoor EKF SLAM $\sim 0.05$–$0.20$ m; outdoor Lidar-SLAM $\sim 0.30$–$1.50$ m.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 Worked Derivation: NEES-Based Consistency Check on an EKF Case Study

This is the **single most asked derivation** in KTU board papers for Module 3 case study questions. We derive the test statistic step-by-step.

### 🎯 Problem Setup

A 2D differential-drive robot uses EKF localization with state $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, \theta]^\top$. Over $N = 50$ Monte Carlo runs, the *j*-th run produces an estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^{(j)}$ with covariance $\Sigma_t^{(j)}$. We want to verify whether the filter is **consistent**.

### 🔢 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the estimation error vector for the *j*-th run.**

$$
\mathbf{e}_t^{(j)} = \mathbf{x}_t^{(j)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^{(j)}
$$

*Logic:* The error is the *true* state minus the filter's *estimate*. If the filter is unbiased, $E[\mathbf{e}_t^{(j)}] = \mathbf{0}$.

---

**Step 2 — Form the quadratic form (Mahalanobis distance) for one run.**

$$
\epsilon_t^{(j)} = \left( \mathbf{e}_t^{(j)} \right)^\top \left( \Sigma_t^{(j)} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{e}_t^{(j)}
$$

*Logic:* This expression measures "how many standard-deviations away" the true state lies, in the metric defined by the filter's covariance. If the covariance is correct, this quantity follows a $\chi^2$ distribution with $r$ degrees of freedom, where $r = \dim(\mathbf{x}_t) = 3$.

---

**Step 3 — Average over all $N$ Monte Carlo runs to get the NEES statistic.**

$$
\bar{\epsilon}_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \epsilon_t^{(j)}
$$

Substituting Step 2:

$$
\bar{\epsilon}_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left( \mathbf{x}_t^{(j)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^{(j)} \right)^\top \left( \Sigma_t^{(j)} \right)^{-1} \left( \mathbf{x}_t^{(j)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^{(j)} \right)
$$

---

**Step 4 — Express the acceptance bounds using the chi-squared distribution.**

For state dimension $r$, under the null hypothesis $H_0$ (filter is consistent):

$$
\bar{\epsilon}_t \sim \frac{1}{N} \chi^2_{Nr}
$$

The two-sided 95% confidence interval is:

$$
\chi^2_{Nr, \, \alpha/2} \le N \cdot \bar{\epsilon}_t \le \chi^2_{Nr, \, 1-\alpha/2}
$$

Equivalently, the bounds on $\bar{\epsilon}_t$ are:

$$
\frac{1}{N} \chi^2_{Nr, \, \alpha/2} \le \bar{\epsilon}_t \le \frac{1}{N} \chi^2_{Nr, \, 1-\alpha/2}
$$

For $N = 50$ and $r = 3$, the degrees of freedom are $Nr = 150$. From chi-squared tables:

$$
\chi^2_{150, \, 0.025} \approx 113.39 \quad ; \quad \chi^2_{150, \, 0.975} \approx 190.51
$$

So the acceptance bounds on $\bar{\epsilon}_t$ are:

$$
\frac{113.39}{50} \le \bar{\epsilon}_t \le \frac{190.51}{50}
$$

$$
2.268 \le \bar{\epsilon}_t \le 3.810
$$

---

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

$$
\text{If } \bar{\epsilon}_t < 2.268 \Rightarrow \text{Filter is } \textbf{over-conservative} \text{ (covariance too large)}
$$

$$
\text{If } 2.268 \le \bar{\epsilon}_t \le 3.810 \Rightarrow \text{Filter is } \textbf{consistent} \text{ (H}_0 \text{ accepted)}
$$

$$
\text{If } \bar{\epsilon}_t > 3.810 \Rightarrow \text{Filter is } \textbf{over-confident} \text{ (covariance too small, optimisation needed)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often forget to *divide* by $N$ when normalising. If you write $113.39 \le \bar{\epsilon}_t \le 190.51$, you will be marked down by 2 marks immediately. Always normalise by $N$.

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Common Case Study Comparisons

| Case Study Aspect | EKF Localization | UKF Localization | Monte Carlo Localization |
|-------------------|------------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| State Representation | Gaussian $(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Gaussian $(\mu, \Sigma)$ | Set of $N$ weighted particles $\{x^{(i)}, w^{(i)}\}$ |
| Computational Cost $O(\cdot)$ | $O(r^2)$ per step | $O(r^3)$ (sigma points) | $O(N \cdot r)$ per step |
| Handles Multi-modal? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Handles Strong Non-linearity? | ❌ First-order only | ✅ Up to 3rd order | ✅ Arbitrary |
| Typical RMSE (indoor) | $0.10$–$0.25$ m | $0.08$–$0.20$ m | $0.05$–$0.30$ m |
| Typical NEES Range | $2.27$–$3.81$ (consistent) | $2.27$–$3.81$ | Computed differently |
| Failure Mode | Linearisation error near poles | Sigma-point collapse | Particle depletion |
| Required Sample Size | 1 (deterministic) | $2r+1$ sigma points | $N \ge 100$ for 2-D, $N \ge 1000$ for 3-D |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define "Case Study Analysis" in the context of Probabilistic Robotics. List any four standard performance metrics used to validate a case study.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *Case Study Analysis* in Probabilistic Robotics is the structured evaluation of a real or simulated robotic system by applying Bayesian inference to its motion and sensor streams, and quantifying the system's localisation, mapping, or SLAM accuracy against ground truth.

**Four standard performance metrics** (½ mark each = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark):

1. **RMSE** — Root Mean Square Error between estimated and true pose.
2. **ATE** — Absolute Trajectory Error; global consistency of the SLAM map.
3. **RPE** — Relative Pose Error; local drift over fixed time windows.
4. **NEES** — Normalised Estimation Error Squared; statistical consistency test via chi-squared distribution.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the Normalised Estimation Error Squared (NEES) test, and why is it important in practical demonstrations of Bayes filters?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **NEES** is a chi-squared statistical test (1 mark) defined as:

$$
\bar{\epsilon}_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} (\mathbf{x}_t^{(j)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^{(j)})^\top (\Sigma_t^{(j)})^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_t^{(j)} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_t^{(j)})
$$

**Importance:** It validates whether the filter's *reported* covariance matches its *actual* error (1 mark). If $\bar{\epsilon}_t$ lies within the chi-squared bounds, the filter is consistent — neither over-confident nor over-conservative. This is critical because an over-confident filter can cause a self-driving car or surgical robot to make unsafe decisions while *believing* it is accurate (1 mark).

---

## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]*

> A differential-drive robot performs **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** inside a 2-D map of dimensions **10 m × 8 m**. It uses a 360° lidar with maximum range **30 m** and standard deviation of range noise **$\sigma_z = 0.05$ m**. The particle filter is initialised with **$N = 500$** uniformly distributed particles.
>
> **(a)** Derive the MCL algorithm mathematically and explain the role of the effective sample size $N_{\text{eff}}$ with the threshold criterion. State what happens if this criterion is ignored. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** During the demonstration, after 20 iterations the particles cluster into two distinct clouds near two similar corridors. Each cloud has an effective weight of $w_1 = 0.49$ and $w_2 = 0.51$. Compute the **entropy** of the belief and interpret whether the robot is localised or kidnapped. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Motion Update (Sampling)** [2 marks]

For each particle $x_{t-1}^{(i)}$, draw a new state from the motion model:

$$
x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, u_t)
$$

For a differential-drive robot with odometry noise ($\sigma_v$ for translational, $\sigma_\gamma$ for rotational):

$$
x_t^{(i)} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1}^{(i)} \\ y_{t-1}^{(i)} \\ \theta_{t-1}^{(i)} \end{bmatrix} \oplus \begin{bmatrix} \hat{v}_t \\ \hat{\omega}_t \Delta t \end{bmatrix} + w_t^{(i)}, \quad w_t^{(i)} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, Q_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Sensor Update (Importance Weighting)** [2 marks]

Assign each particle an importance weight using the lidar measurement $z_t$:

$$
w_t^{(i)} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)
$$

For a beam-based range finder with likelihood field, the per-beam probability is:

$$
p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_z^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(z_t^k - z_{\text{expected}}^k)^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right)
$$

The total weight is the product over all beams:

$$
p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_z^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(z_t^k - z_{\text{expected}}^k)^2}{2\sigma_z^2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Normalisation** [1 mark]

$$
w_t^{(i)} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{(j)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective Sample Size & Resampling Trigger** [2 marks]

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (w_t^{(i)})^2}
$$

Threshold criterion: Resample **iff** $N_{\text{eff}} < N/2$.

**Consequence of ignoring the criterion:** All particles eventually collapse onto a single wrong hypothesis (particle depletion), or one particle obtains weight $\approx 1$ and the filter becomes unrecoverable from **kidnapping** or **sensor failure**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Normalise the weights** [1 mark]

The weights $w_1 = 0.49$ and $w_2 = 0.51$ are already normalised since $0.49 + 0.51 = 1.00$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Shannon Entropy** [3 marks]

$$
H(\text{bel}) = - \sum_{i=1}^{M} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

Substituting:

$$
H(\text{bel}) = - \left( 0.49 \log_2 0.49 + 0.51 \log_2 0.51 \right)
$$

Compute each term:

$$
0.49 \log_2 0.49 = 0.49 \times (-1.0291) = -0.5043
$$

$$
0.51 \log_2 0.51 = 0.51 \times (-0.9714) = -0.4954
$$

Therefore:

$$
H(\text{bel}) = - (-0.5043 - 0.4954) = 0.9997 \text{ bits} \approx 1.0 \text{ bit}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation** [3 marks]

- The **maximum possible entropy** for $M = 2$ hypotheses is $\log_2 2 = 1.0$ bit.
- Our value $H \approx 1.0$ bit is **at the maximum**, meaning the two corridors are *equally likely* and the robot cannot distinguish between them (this is the classic **"corridor symmetry"** or **perceptual aliasing** problem).
- The robot is **NOT localised**; it is effectively **kidnapped between two symmetric hypotheses**.
- **Practical remedy:** Inject random particles uniformly over the map (MCL recovery from kidnapping) or use a *sensor with higher resolution* (e.g., RGB-D camera) to break the symmetry.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

> An autonomous ground vehicle performs **EKF-based SLAM** on a 2-D map with **50 landmarks**. The motion model has noise covariance $\mathbf{Q} = \text{diag}([0.1^2, 0.1^2, 0.05^2])$ and the measurement model has noise covariance $\mathbf{R} = \text{diag}([0.3^2, 0.3^2])$.
>
> **(a)** Formulate the EKF-SLAM prediction and update equations. Show explicitly the dimensions of the Jacobian matrices $\mathbf{G}_t$ and $\mathbf{H}_t$, assuming the state vector contains the robot pose plus all landmark positions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** During the practical demonstration, the NEES statistic over $N = 50$ Monte Carlo runs is found to be $\bar{\epsilon}_t = 4.25$. Using $r = 3$ and $\alpha = 0.05$, determine whether the filter is consistent, over-conservative, or over-confident. Show all chi-squared calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Define the state vector** [1 mark]

$$
\mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \underbrace{x, y, \theta}_{\text{Robot pose}} \; , \; \underbrace{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_{50}}_{\text{Landmark positions}} \end{bmatrix}^\top
$$

Each landmark $m_i = [m_{ix}, m_{iy}]^\top$. Total state dimension:

$$
\dim(\mathbf{x}_t) = 3 + 2 \times 50 = 103
$$

**Step 2 — Prediction step** [3 marks]

$$
\bar{\mu}_t = \mathbf{x}_{t-1} \oplus (u_t + w_t), \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q})
$$

Covariance propagation:

$$
\bar{\Sigma}_t = \mathbf{G}_t \Sigma_{t-1} \mathbf{G}_t^\top + \mathbf{Q}_t
$$

where $\mathbf{G}_t = \dfrac{\partial g(u_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})}{\partial \mathbf{x}_{t-1}}$ is the **Jacobian of the motion model**.

**Dimensions:** $\mathbf{G}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{103 \times 103}$, $\Sigma_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{103 \times 103}$, $\mathbf{Q}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{103 \times 103}$ (padded with zeros for landmarks).

**Step 3 — Update step** [3 marks]

When landmark $j$ is observed:

$$
\mathbf{K}_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \mathbf{H}_t^\top (\mathbf{H}_t \bar{\Sigma}_t \mathbf{H}_t^\top + \mathbf{R}_t)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + \mathbf{K}_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))
$$

$$
\Sigma_t = (I - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t
$$

where $\mathbf{H}_t = \dfrac{\partial h(\mathbf{x}_t)}{\partial \mathbf{x}_t}$ is the **Jacobian of the measurement model**.

**Dimensions:** For a single landmark observation:

- $\mathbf{H}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 103}$
- $\mathbf{R}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$
- $\mathbf{K}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{103 \times 2}$

The computational complexity per update is dominated by the inverse, $O(2^3)$, but the **full matrix multiplication** is $O(103^2 \cdot 2)$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Degrees of freedom** [1 mark]

$$
\nu = N \cdot r = 50 \times 3 = 150
$$

**Step 2 — Chi-squared bounds** [2 marks]

For $\alpha = 0.05$ two-sided test:

$$
\chi^2_{150, \, 0.025} \approx 113.39 \quad ; \quad \chi^2_{150, \, 0.975} \approx 190.51
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the normalised statistic** [2 marks]

$$
N \cdot \bar{\epsilon}_t = 50 \times 4.25 = 212.5
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the test** [2 marks]

$$
N \cdot \bar{\epsilon}_t = 212.5 \; > \; \chi^2_{150, \, 0.975} = 190.51
$$

Therefore the test statistic **exceeds the upper bound**. The filter is **over-confident**, meaning the reported covariance $\Sigma_t$ is **smaller than the true error**. The robot's internal belief is *more certain than reality*.

**Practical remedy:** Increase the process noise covariance $\mathbf{Q}$ and/or the measurement noise covariance $\mathbf{R}$ until NEES falls inside the acceptance interval $[2.268, \, 3.810]$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks
>
> 1. **Forgetting to normalise NEES by $N$** — always divide by $N$ before comparing against the bound. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Using raw dimensions for Jacobian matrices** — examiners expect explicit $m \times n$ notation. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Ignoring the "interpret" step** — every metric calculation *must* end with a one-line physical interpretation. **−2 marks** if omitted.
> 4. **Confusing ATE vs. RPE** — ATE is global (full trajectory), RPE is local (windowed). Mixing them up costs **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Writing the entropy base wrong** — use $\log_2$ for bits, $\ln$ for nats. State the base explicitly. **−1 mark.**
> 6. **Not mentioning the **kidnapping recovery** mechanism in MCL answers — this is a frequent 7-mark sub-part. Always include the "inject random particles" line.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Case Study** in Module 3 = a structured application of the **Bayes filter** to a real or simulated robot, validated against quantitative metrics.
- ✅ The **5-stage pipeline** is: *Identify → Extract → Choose → Compute → Validate*. Memorise this order.
- ✅ The three pillars of any case study are: **Motion Model $p(x_t \vert x_{t-1}, u_t)$**, **Sensor Model $p(z_t \vert x_t)$**, and **Belief $bel(x_t)$**.
- ✅ Performance is judged by **RMSE** (accuracy), **ATE/RPE** (trajectory consistency), and **NEES** (statistical consistency).
- ✅ **EKF** suits linear-Gaussian, **UKF** suits mild non-linearity, **Particle Filter** suits multi-modal/non-Gaussian beliefs.
- ✅ The **effective sample size** $N_{\text{eff}}$ is the *single most important* diagnostic in any particle-filter case study. Threshold = $N/2$.
- ✅ **Perceptual aliasing** (corridor symmetry) is the most common reason MCL fails. Always discuss remedies (inject random particles, richer sensors).
- ✅ **NEES interpretation:** below bound → *over-conservative*; inside bound → *consistent*; above bound → *over-confident*.
- ✅ The **chi-squared bounds** for $N = 50$, $r = 3$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ are $[2.268, \, 3.810]$ — *memorise this range*.
- ✅ Always quote a **physical interpretation** of any metric; numerical answers without interpretation lose marks.
- ✅ **Real-world datasets** to remember: **Victoria Park** (outdoor), **KITTI** (driving), **EuRoC** (MAV), **MIT-CSAIL** (indoor).
- ✅ KTU examiners favour **scenario-style numerical questions** (10 × 8 m room, 500 particles, 50 landmarks) over abstract theory. Practise with concrete numbers.
- ✅ The covariance matrices **$\mathbf{Q}$** (motion) and **$\mathbf{R}$** (sensor) must be tuned when NEES fails — this is the *only* correct practical remedy in board answers.
- ✅ Avoid Python or code in your answer sheets for this course (unless explicitly an algorithm-design elective). Stick to equations and matrices.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Final Project Integrating Learned Probabilistic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT863 - PROBABILISTIC ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Final Project Integrating Learned Probabilistic Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Project — Integration of Probabilistic Algorithms (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A capstone engineering artefact in which the student designs, mathematically formulates, simulates, and evaluates a **unified robotic perception–cognition stack** that fuses at least three probabilistic algorithms studied in Modules 1–3 of PERAT863 (e.g., Bayesian Filter, Kalman/Extended Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, MCL, EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Occupancy-Grid Mapping) into a **single coherent pipeline** that solves a real-world robotics problem (localization, mapping, autonomous navigation, or object tracking).

The project must demonstrate three cardinal competencies expected by the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework:

1. **Algorithmic synthesis** — combining complementary filters (e.g., EKF for pose tracking + PF for global relocalization + Graph-SLAM for loop closure).
2. **Probabilistic correctness** — every module is justified through Bayes' rule, Markov assumption, and explicit uncertainty propagation.
3. **Quantitative evaluation** — reporting Root Mean Square Error (**RMSE**), Effective Sample Size (**N_eff**), Computational Time per Cycle (**CTC** in **ms**), and Success Rate (**SR** in **%**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "GPS + Radar + Compass" Analogy**
> Imagine driving a car in an unfamiliar city at night. Your **GPS** (Kalman Filter) gives a smooth but drifting estimate. Your **visual landmarks** (Particle Filter candidates) anchor you to known intersections. Your **magnetic compass** (compass/IMU + EKF) corrects heading errors. None of these alone is sufficient — but the car's navigation system **fuses** all three, weighting each by its measured uncertainty.
>
> A "Final Probabilistic Robotics Project" is exactly that fusion — but for a robot — where the **uncertainty weights** (variances, particle weights, belief entropies) are computed *algorithmically* using Bayes' theorem rather than tuned by hand.

## 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlights

- The project must include at least **one filtering algorithm**, **one localization algorithm**, and **one mapping/SLAM component** working in concert.
- Implementation may use **ROS 2 / Gazebo / MATLAB Robotics Toolbox / Python (NumPy/SciPy)** simulation environments (CS-eligible topic; code is allowed in this course).
- Mandatory deliverable: a **2-page report** + **10-minute viva** demonstrating mathematical correctness and a working demonstration (video or live).
- The integration must be tested on at least one **benchmark dataset** (e.g., Victoria Park, UTIAS, or the KTU-provided `.bag` files) with **N ≥ 5 trials**.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Universally Required)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation**
> - **RMSE** — Root Mean Square Error of estimated pose vs. ground truth.
> - **NEES** — Normalized Estimation Error Squared (consistency test).
> - **N_eff** — Effective Sample Size of particle set.
> - **CTC** — Computational Time per Cycle in **ms**.
> - **ATE** — Absolute Trajectory Error in **meters**.
> - **SR** — Success Rate in **%** of trials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architectural Philosophy of Integration

A successful final project does **not** concatenate algorithms. It composes them through the **Bayes Filter abstraction** as a *meta-algorithm*. Each concrete filter (KF, EKF, UKF, PF) is a *specialization* of one universal recursion. The integrator's job is to choose the right specialization per sub-task and ensure **information flow** is consistent.

The project design follows a strict **5-stage pipeline**:

| Stage | Name | Purpose | Key Algorithms from PERAT863 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Perception** | Convert raw sensor streams $\to$ probabilistic measurements | Beam model, Likelihood field, Feature extractors |
| 2 | **Prediction** | Propagate belief through motion model | Velocity model, Odometry model |
| 3 | **Update** | Fuse prediction with new measurement via Bayes | Kalman gain, Importance weighting |
| 4 | **Mapping / SLAM** | Build/Update environment representation | Occupancy grid, EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM |
| 5 | **Decision / Control** | Act on the posterior belief | PID, MPC, Policy over belief |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU Examiners**
> The KTU 2024 examiner awards full marks only when the student can articulate *which* sub-problem is solved by *which* specialization of the Bayes filter, and *why* that choice is mathematically optimal (linearity, modality, dimensionality).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Algorithm Selection (Engineering Justification)

- **Use Kalman / EKF** when the system is **approximately linear-Gaussian**: motion is smooth, sensor noise is Gaussian, and the posterior is unimodal. *Example*: indoor wheeled robot with wheel-odometry + LIDAR point-to-point.
- **Use Particle Filter (MCL)** when the posterior is **multi-modal** (global localization, "kidnapped robot") or the model is **highly non-Gaussian**.
- **Use EKF-SLAM** for small maps (≤ ~50 landmarks) where the augmented state covariance is tractable.
- **Use FastSLAM** for large maps with many landmarks — uses Rao-Blackwellization: pose as PF, landmarks as independent EKFs.
- **Use Graph-SLAM** when loop closures dominate the optimization budget and the map must be refined globally.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in either Module Test 2 or End Semester. Memorize the units and the normalizer $\eta$.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Specialization |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior belief | **Universal Bayes Filter** |
| 2 | $\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | Prediction step | All filters |
| 3 | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | Update step | All filters |
| 4 | $\eta = \left[\int p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)\, dx_t\right]^{-1}$ | Normalizer | All filters |
| 5 | $\bar{x}_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | KF mean prediction | **Linear Gaussian** |
| 6 | $\bar{P}_t = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^{\top} + R_t$ | KF covariance prediction | **Linear Gaussian** |
| 7 | $K_t = \bar{P}_t C_t^{\top}\left(C_t \bar{P}_t C_t^{\top} + Q_t\right)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | KF / EKF |
| 8 | $x_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t \left(z_t - C_t \bar{x}_t\right)$ | KF state update | KF / EKF |
| 9 | $P_t = (I - K_t C_t)\, \bar{P}_t$ | KF covariance update | KF / EKF |
| 10 | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})\, w_{t-1}^{[i]}$ | Particle weight update | **PF / MCL** |
| 11 | $N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Effective sample size | **PF / MCL** |
| 12 | $x_t^{[i]} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Proposal distribution | **PF / EKF-PF** |
| 13 | $\bar{\mu}_{j,t} = \bar{\mu}_{j,t-1}$ ; $\bar{\Sigma}_{j,t} = \Sigma_{j,t-1} + R_t$ | Landmark EKF prediction | **FastSLAM** |
| 14 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \left(\hat{x}_k - x_k\right)^{\top}\left(\hat{x}_k - x_k\right)}$ | Trajectory error | **All projects** |
| 15 | $\epsilon_k = (x_k - \hat{x}_k)^{\top} P_k^{-1} (x_k - \hat{x}_k)$ | NEES (consistency) | **KF / EKF / PF** |
| 16 | $\text{ATE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \left\Vert \mathbf{p}_k^{\text{est}} - \mathbf{p}_k^{\text{gt}}\right\Vert^2}$ | Absolute trajectory error | **SLAM projects** |
| 17 | $z_t^j = \begin{pmatrix}\sqrt{(m_{x,j}-x)^2+(m_{y,j}-y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{y,j}-y, m_{x,j}-x)-\theta\end{pmatrix} + \nu_t$ | Range-bearing observation | **EKF-SLAM** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Trick**
> In equation (7), many students write the **transpose** in the wrong place. The correct form is $C_t \bar{P}_t C_t^{\top} + Q_t$ inside the inversion. $Q_t$ is the **measurement noise covariance**, $R_t$ is the **process noise covariance**. Confusing them is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in Module-4 questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla FSD)**: Multi-sensor fusion = EKF (IMU+GPS) + PF (lane/landmark) + Graph-SLAM (HD-map refinement).
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Locus Robotics)**: MCL for global relocalization + Occupancy grid for obstacle avoidance + EKF for wheel-odometry smoothing.
- **Aerial drones (DJI, Skydio)**: UKF for attitude estimation + FastSLAM for visual-inertial mapping.
- **Underwater ROVs**: PF for acoustic-SLAM (highly non-Gaussian multipath).
- **Medical robotics (da Vinci)**: EKF for tool-tip pose + PF for tissue tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away for KTU Viva**
> Always cite **one real-world deployment** of your chosen algorithm stack. Examiners award up to **2 grace marks** for industry relevance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical foundation** required to defend a final-project design in the KTU viva. No steps are omitted; every transition is justified.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I: The Universal Bayesian Filter Recursion (Foundation of All Integration)

**Goal:** Starting from the definition of belief, derive the two-step recursion (predict-then-update) that unifies KF, EKF, and PF.

**Step 1 — Definition of belief.**

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where $z_{1:t} = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_t)$ are measurements and $u_{1:t} = (u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_t)$ are controls.

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' rule (condition on the *new* measurement $z_t$ only).**

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 3 — Measurement Markov assumption** ($z_t$ depends only on $x_t$):

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

**Step 4 — Total probability over $x_{t-1}$:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 — State Markov assumption** ($x_t$ depends on $x_{t-1}$ and $u_t$ only):

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$$

**Step 6 — Control Markov** ($x_{t-1}$ does not depend on $u_t$):

$$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \;=\; \text{bel}(x_{t-1})$$

**Step 7 — Combine Steps 5 + 6 into Step 4 (Prediction):**

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) \;=\; p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute into Step 3 (Update):**

$$\text{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Implication**
> Equations (Step 7) and (Step 8) are the *seam* through which any algorithm can be plugged in. KF/EKF satisfy the integrals in closed form; PF approximates them via Monte-Carlo samples. Your final project must explicitly identify which sub-step is solved by which algorithm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) — the Backbone of EKF-SLAM

**Step 1 — Nonlinear system model:**

$$x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

$$z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

**Step 2 — First-order Taylor expansion around $(\mu_{t-1}, \bar{\mu}_t)$:**

$$g(u_t, x_{t-1}) \approx g(u_t, \mu_{t-1}) + G_t (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$$

where $G_t = \dfrac{\partial g(u_t, x_{t-1})}{\partial x_{t-1}}\bigg\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}$ is the **Jacobian**.

**Step 3 — Mean prediction:**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$$

**Step 4 — Covariance prediction:**

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^{\top} + R_t$$

**Step 5 — Linearized observation:**

$$h(x_t) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t), \quad H_t = \frac{\partial h(x_t)}{\partial x_t}\bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$$

**Step 6 — Kalman gain (using linearized $H_t$):**

$$K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{\top} \left(H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{\top} + Q_t\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — State update (mean):**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \left[z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)\right]$$

**Step 8 — State update (covariance):**

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\, \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why EKF is the integrator's workhorse**
> In EKF-SLAM the state vector is augmented to $x_t = (x, y, \theta, m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n)^{\top}$, and the covariance $\Sigma_t$ grows as $\mathcal{O}((3+2n)^2)$. The Jacobian $G_t$ becomes block-diagonal (robot block + landmark blocks) — this is exactly the structure exploited in **FastSLAM** via Rao-Blackwellization.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Particle Filter Resampling (MCL Engine for Project's Global-Localization Module)

**Step 1 — Posterior approximated by $M$ weighted samples:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]}\, \delta\left(x_t - x_t^{[i]}\right), \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Importance sampling from proposal $q = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{[i]}, u_t)$ (the motion model):**

$$w_t^{[i]} \;\propto\; w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$$

**Step 3 — Closed-form weight update (dropping the constant $\eta$ per particle):**

$$w_t^{[i]} \;=\; \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})\, w_{t-1}^{[i]}$$

**Step 4 — Effective sample size (Kish's formula):**

$$N_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M}\left(w_t^{[i]}\right)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Resampling trigger condition (used in the integrated project):**

$$\text{If } N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Resample via Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)}$$

**Step 6 — Resampling step (output is unweighted set $\{x_t^{[i]}, 1/M\}$):**

$$x_t^{[i]} \sim \sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}\, \delta\left(x_t - x_t^{[j]}\right) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, M$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Integration Hook**
> In the final project, the **resampling trigger** is gated by the SLAM module: if the map-loop-closure detector reports a successful match, $N_{\text{threshold}}$ is *raised* (e.g., from $0.5 M$ to $0.8 M$) to preserve diversity, preventing particle collapse during global relocalization.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: RMSE and Consistency (NEES) for Final-Project Evaluation

**Step 1 — Per-timestep error vector:**

$$e_k = x_k^{\text{est}} - x_k^{\text{gt}}$$

**Step 2 — RMSE (most commonly reported metric in KTU evaluations):**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} e_k^{\top} e_k} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[(x_k^{\text{est}}-x_k^{\text{gt}})^2 + (y_k^{\text{est}}-y_k^{\text{gt}})^2\right]}$$

**Step 3 — NEES for a single run (tests filter self-consistency):**

$$\epsilon_k = e_k^{\top} \Sigma_k^{-1} e_k$$

**Step 4 — Average NEES over N timesteps:**

$$\bar{\epsilon} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \epsilon_k$$

**Step 5 — Chi-squared 95 % acceptance bounds (KTU-rubric standard):**

$$\bar{\epsilon} \in \left[\chi^2_{d, 0.025},\; \chi^2_{d, 0.975}\right]$$

where $d$ is the state dimension. For 2D pose, $d = 3$, bounds are approximately $[0.216,\; 9.348]$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake (KTU examiner's note)**
> A filter can have low RMSE yet be *inconsistent* (over-confident). Always report both RMSE *and* NEES. A consistent filter satisfies the chi-squared bound; an inconsistent one is silently failing.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm-to-Project-Component Mapping

| Project Component | Algorithm Choice | Mathematical Justification | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wheel-odometry smoothing | EKF | Linear-Gaussian, low-dim | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ per step |
| Global relocalization (kidnap recovery) | MCL (PF) | Multi-modal posterior | $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot n)$ |
| Static landmark mapping | EKF-SLAM | Small map, linearized obs. | $\mathcal{O}((3+2n)^3)$ |
| Large-scale outdoor SLAM | FastSLAM | Rao-Blackwellization | $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot n \cdot k^2)$ |
| Occupancy grid (LIDAR) | Log-odds Bayes filter | Independent cells | $\mathcal{O}(W \cdot H)$ |
| Loop closure | Graph-SLAM | Sparse optimization | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ once |
| Consistency check | NEES / Chi-square | Statistical test | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ |
| Performance report | RMSE / ATE | $\ell_2$ norm average | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot d)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Project Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **integrated architecture** of a final project that fuses Bayesian filtering, Kalman-based tracking, Particle-Filter localization, and SLAM. The flow is **closed-loop** — outputs feed back into the next sensor cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sensorA[Sensor Input LIDAR IMU GPS] --> perceptionB[Perception Module]
    perceptionB --> featureC[Feature Extraction Landmarks Beams]
    featureC --> algoSelD{Algorithm Selection}
    algoSelD -->|Linear Gaussian| kalmanE[Kalman Filter Pose Smoothing]
    algoSelD -->|Nonlinear Multimodal| ekfF[Extended Kalman Filter EKF SLAM]
    algoSelD -->|Global Relocalization| particleG[Particle Filter MCL]
    kalmanE --> stateH[State Estimator bel x t]
    ekfF --> stateH
    particleG --> stateH
    stateH --> locI[Localization Module]
    stateH --> slamJ[SLAM Module]
    slamJ --> loopCloseK[Loop Closure Detector]
    loopCloseK -->|Yes| graphL[Graph Optimization Pose Graph]
    loopCloseK -->|No| slamJ
    graphL --> slamJ
    locI --> mapM[Map Integration Occupancy Grid]
    slamJ --> mapM
    mapM --> planN[Path Planner A Star RRT]
    planN --> ctrlO[Motion Controller PID MPC]
    ctrlO --> actP[Robot Actuator]
    actP --> sensorA
    stateH --> metricQ[Performance Logger RMSE NEES N eff CTC]
    mapM --> metricQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU Viva tip)**
> - The **triangle** `algoSelD` is the design-decision point — the student must justify, with equations, *why* a given branch is taken.
> - The **back-edge** `actP --> sensorA` is the closed-loop feedback essential for recursive Bayes filtering.
> - The **side-channel** to `metricQ` is mandatory for the KTU rubric: every cycle logs RMSE, NEES, $N_{\text{eff}}$, and CTC.

## 4.2 Algorithm-Data-Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    motionModelA[Motion Model u t] --> predictB[Predict bel bar x t]
    prevBelC[Previous Belief bel x t-1] --> predictB
    predictB --> priorD[Prior bel bar x t]
    measurementE[Measurement z t] --> likelihoodF[Likelihood p z t given x t]
    priorD --> updateG[Bayes Update]
    likelihoodF --> updateG
    updateG --> posteriorH[Posterior bel x t]

```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Two questions are provided, both directly aligned with the integrative final-project theme. Model answers are concise but mathematically precise.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper Set B)]
**Define an "integrated probabilistic robotics project". Justify why a single algorithm (KF or PF alone) is insufficient for real-world autonomous navigation, citing two distinct scenarios.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An integrated probabilistic robotics project is a unified software stack that combines at least three Bayesian-filter specializations — typically an EKF (or UKF) for short-term pose tracking, a Particle Filter (MCL) for global relocalization, and a SLAM algorithm (EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, or Graph-SLAM) for concurrent mapping — to solve a real-world task such as autonomous navigation, object tracking, or exploration.

- **Scenario 1 (why KF alone fails):** A robot started at an unknown position in a symmetric corridor (e.g., long office hallway) will produce a *multi-modal* posterior over (x, y). The KF's Gaussian assumption collapses to the wrong peak and the robot becomes permanently lost.
- **Scenario 2 (why PF alone is wasteful):** In a well-tracked trajectory (low-uncertainty unimodal posterior), running M = 1000 particles is needlessly expensive. A simple EKF achieves the same accuracy at $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ vs. PF's $\mathcal{O}(M n)$.

Hence integration — using PF only when multi-modality is detected — is the principled solution. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Effective Sample Size ($N_{\text{eff}}$) in a particle filter? Explain its role in an integrated project where the particle-filter module co-exists with an EKF-SLAM module.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The effective sample size is defined as:

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \left(w_t^{[i]}\right)^2}$$

It quantifies how *uniform* the particle weights are. When $N_{\text{eff}} \approx M$, all particles contribute equally (healthy diversity). When $N_{\text{eff}} \to 1$, a single particle dominates (degeneracy).

**Role in integration:** $N_{\text{eff}}$ acts as a **gating signal** between the PF and the EKF-SLAM module. Specifically, the project uses:

$$N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{trigger resampling}$$

and conversely, when the EKF-SLAM loop-closure detector reports a successful match, $N_{\text{threshold}}$ is dynamically raised (e.g., from $0.5 M$ to $0.8 M$) to *prevent* premature resampling, preserving particle diversity for the next global-relocalization challenge. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. *Both* alternative choices (Question A and Question B) are provided below. The student attempts **one** of them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Design the architecture of a final project that integrates Kalman filtering, particle filtering, and EKF-SLAM. Justify the data-flow and synchronization between modules. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture is a **closed-loop, three-tier pipeline**:

**Tier 1 — Local Pose Tracking (EKF module).** Processes high-rate IMU + wheel-odometry (e.g., 100 Hz). State: $x_t = (x, y, \theta, \dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})^{\top}$, with linearized motion and observation models. The EKF publishes pose mean $\mu_t$ and covariance $\Sigma_t$ to Tier 2 at a reduced rate (10 Hz).

**Tier 2 — Global Localization (Particle-Filter / MCL module).** Activated when the EKF's trace$(\Sigma_t) > \sigma_{\text{threshold}}^2$ (i.e., the EKF has lost confidence). Particles are sampled from the EKF's proposal $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$, and re-weighted against LIDAR scan-matching. Resampling is triggered by $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5 M$. Once $N_{\text{eff}} \approx M$, Tier 2 hands control back to Tier 1.

**Tier 3 — Mapping (EKF-SLAM module).** Operates in parallel; augments the EKF state with landmark positions when they are first observed. Loop-closure events (verified by geometric consistency) trigger a Tier 1 $\to$ Tier 2 reset, after which Tier 2 re-initializes particles around the loop-closed pose.

**Synchronization:** A ROS 2 *tf-tree* (or equivalent message bus) propagates the current best pose estimate with an associated uncertainty stamp. Each module subscribes to the others' outputs with a time-stamp filter to prevent using stale measurements. **(7 marks — full credit requires a labelled block diagram and the gating rule involving $\text{trace}(\Sigma_t)$ and $N_{\text{eff}}$).**

#### (b) Derive the Bayesian-filter update step from first principles. Show explicitly how the result becomes the foundation for integrating KF, EKF, and PF in a single project. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

See Section 3.1 of these notes. The student is expected to reproduce **all eight steps** of the derivation, leading to:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

**Integration insight (additional 2–3 marks):** The two-step recursion is a *meta-algorithm*. The integral in the prediction step is computed:

- **In closed form** by KF/EKF (Gaussian $\otimes$ Gaussian = Gaussian).
- **By Monte-Carlo sampling** by PF: $\int f(x)\, p(x)\, dx \approx \frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M} f(x^{[i]})$.

Thus a single project that uses EKF on Tier 1 (closed form) and PF on Tier 2 (Monte-Carlo) is mathematically consistent — both are evaluating the *same* Bayes recursion, just with different numerical engines. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the integration of Monte-Carlo Localization (MCL) with occupancy-grid mapping. Discuss the challenges of real-time implementation and propose two solutions. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

In the integrated system, the particle filter maintains a distribution over robot poses, while the occupancy grid maintains a 2-D map of cell-wise occupation probabilities. The two interact as follows:

**Forward pass (per particle $i$):** Sample a pose $x_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$. Then for each LIDAR beam $b = 1, \ldots, B$, ray-cast from $x_t^{[i]}$ through the current global map to obtain the expected endpoint; compare with the actual measurement to compute the per-beam likelihood. The aggregated log-likelihood is added to the particle weight.

**Reverse pass (per particle $i$):** Update the cell probabilities along each beam using the log-odds formulation:

$$l_t(m) = l_{t-1}(m) + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m, x_t, z_t) - l_0$$

**Challenges and solutions:**

1. **Computational cost:** Ray-casting $B$ beams through $W \times H$ cells for $M$ particles costs $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot B \cdot D)$ where $D$ is the maximum ray length. **Solution:** Use a *distance-map* (chamfer map) precomputed once per grid update, reducing ray-casting from $\mathcal{O}(D)$ to $\mathcal{O}(1)$ via a lookup.

2. **Map-particle mismatch:** Each particle has historically used *its own* map copy (Augmented-MCL), which is memory-prohibitive for large maps. **Solution:** Share a *single* global map for all particles; the localization-only likelihood uses a low-resolution coarser map, while the final pose refines a high-resolution map post-localization.

**(7 marks — full credit requires explicit cost expressions and a brief note on the coarser-map heuristic.)**

#### (b) Derive the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) prediction and update steps for a nonlinear system. Show the differences from the standard Kalman filter and explain the conditions under which the EKF approximation is valid. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

See Section 3.2 of these notes. The student reproduces the eight-step derivation.

**Differences from standard KF:**

| Aspect | KF | EKF |
|---|---|---|
| Motion model | $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t$ | $x_t = g(u_t, x_{t-1}) + \epsilon_t$ |
| Observation model | $z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t$ | $z_t = h(x_t) + \delta_t$ |
| Mean prediction | $\bar{x}_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$ |
| Covariance prediction | $\bar{P}_t = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^{\top} + R_t$ | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^{\top} + R_t$ |
| Kalman gain | $K_t = \bar{P}_t C_t^{\top}(C_t \bar{P}_t C_t^{\top} + Q_t)^{-1}$ | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{\top}(H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{\top} + Q_t)^{-1}$ |
| Innovation | $z_t - C_t \bar{x}_t$ (linear) | $z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ (nonlinear residual) |

**Validity conditions:** The first-order Taylor expansion is accurate when the residual $(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$ remains small (i.e., the filter is *locally linear*). In practice, this requires:

- Low process noise (smooth dynamics), or
- High measurement rate (so $\Sigma_{t-1}$ stays small), and
- Smooth nonlinearities (no discontinuities in $g$ or $h$).

If any of these break, the EKF diverges and PF or UKF should be substituted. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Module-4 Final-Project Questions**
> 1. **Confusing $R_t$ (process noise) and $Q_t$ (measurement noise).** $R_t$ enters the *prediction* covariance, $Q_t$ enters the *update* (Kalman-gain denominator). Swap them → estimator diverges → 0 marks for the numerical answer.
> 2. **Omitting the normalizer $\eta$ in the Bayes update.** Full marks require the explicit statement that $\eta = [\int p(z_t \mid x_t)\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)\, dx_t]^{-1}$, not just the proportionality symbol $\propto$.
> 3. **Reporting only RMSE without NEES/chi-square.** A filter can be biased *and* over-confident. Always include the consistency test.
> 4. **No justification of algorithm choice.** Choosing "PF because it's modern" scores 0; choosing "PF because the posterior is multi-modal in the kidnap-recovery scenario" scores full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the units.** RMSE in **meters**, CTC in **ms**, SR in **%**, $N_{\text{eff}}$ *dimensionless* in $[1, M]$.
> 6. **Skipping the resampling-trigger derivation.** $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}}$ is not a heuristic — it follows from Kish's sampling theory and the variance bound on importance weights.
> 7. **Reporting a single trial.** KTU rubric requires $N \geq 5$ trials and a mean ± std-dev band.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Final-Project Integration: 12-Point Revision Bullets**
> 1. The **Bayes filter** is the *meta-algorithm*; KF, EKF, UKF, PF are *specializations*.
> 2. **Prediction equation** is a Chapman–Kolmogorov integral; **Update equation** is a point-wise multiplication followed by normalization.
> 3. **Kalman filter** = closed-form solution under linear-Gaussian assumptions.
> 4. **EKF** = first-order Taylor-linearized KF for *mildly* nonlinear systems.
> 5. **Particle filter** = Monte-Carlo approximation of the Bayes recursion for *any* distribution.
> 6. **$N_{\text{eff}}$** is the only principled resampling trigger; do not use a fixed time-step.
> 7. **EKF-SLAM** augments the state with landmark positions; complexity $\mathcal{O}((3+2n)^3)$.
> 8. **FastSLAM** uses Rao-Blackwellization: one PF for pose, independent EKFs per landmark.
> 9. **Graph-SLAM** is the *global-optimization* layer; it is *not* a filter, it is a batch solver.
> 10. **NEES chi-square test** is the *only* way to verify filter consistency — RMSE alone is insufficient.
> 11. **Algorithm selection rule of thumb:** unimodal + smooth → KF/EKF; multi-modal or highly non-Gaussian → PF; small map → EKF-SLAM; large map with many landmarks → FastSLAM.
> 12. **Project deliverables (KTU 2024):** working demo + 2-page report + 10-min viva + NEES/RMSE plots + $N \geq 5$ trials.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — PERAT863 Probabilistic Robotics (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Research Paper Review and Presentation** is a structured academic exercise in which a student critically analyzes, synthesizes, and orally communicates the findings, methodology, contributions, and limitations of one or more contemporary (typically last 3–5 years) peer-reviewed research articles published in top-tier venues relevant to the course domain. Under the **PERAT863 – Probabilistic Robotics** syllabus (Module 4), this activity is the capstone evaluative component that tests the learner's ability to engage with state-of-the-art literature on probabilistic methods for robot perception, localization, mapping, planning, and learning under uncertainty.

In the context of **Probabilistic Robotics** (drawing from seminal works by Thrun, Burgard, Fox and contemporary extensions), a *contemporary research paper* typically addresses advances in one or more of the following sub-domains:

- Bayesian filtering (Kalman, Extended Kalman, Unscented Kalman, Particle Filters)
- SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) — visual, LiDAR, graph-based, semantic
- Probabilistic planning under uncertainty (POMDPs, chance-constrained planning)
- Deep probabilistic models for robotics (Bayesian Neural Networks, Normalizing Flows, Diffusion Models)
- Multi-robot probabilistic coordination and belief space planning
- Uncertainty-aware perception (object detection, depth estimation, sensor fusion)

The **review** demands a *critical appraisal* — not merely a summary — of the paper's hypothesis, mathematical modeling, experimental validation, statistical significance, and reproducibility. The **presentation** transforms this critical review into a **time-bounded, visually rich, peer-audience-friendly** oral discourse, typically 15–20 minutes in KTU evaluation settings.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Courtroom Expert Witness**
> 
> Imagine you are an **expert witness in a courtroom**. A research paper is like a suspect on trial. Your job is *not* to worship the paper's claims, but to put it on the stand and interrogate it:
> 
> - **What is it claiming?** (the hypothesis / contribution)
> - **What evidence does it present?** (the methodology, datasets, experiments)
> - **Is the evidence sound?** (statistical rigor, baselines, ablations)
> - **Are there hidden flaws?** (assumptions, cherry-picking, reproducibility)
> - **Can the verdict hold up in the wider scientific community?** (citations, follow-up work, generalizability)
> 
> After the interrogation, you must then **stand before a jury (your classmates and faculty)** and deliver a clear, fair, and impactful summary of your findings — that is the **presentation**.

The **robot** in probabilistic robotics is, in essence, a *rational agent* trying to reason under uncertainty using probability distributions. A **contemporary research paper** is a *new piece of evidence* (a new sensor, a new filter, a new benchmark) that updates the community's belief about how robots should perceive and act. Your review **updates the belief** of the audience about that paper.

---

### 1.3 Physical / Standard Constants in This Module

Although this module is not a *numerical computation* module, the following **standardized evaluation metrics** are universally used in probabilistic robotics research and **must** be understood by a student presenting such work:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Probabilistic Robotics Evaluation Metrics (must be recognized in any paper review)**
> 
> - **ATE** (Absolute Trajectory Error) — measured in **meters (m)** or **RMSE (m)**
> - **RPE** (Relative Pose Error) — per-meter drift, measured in **m/m** or **%**
> - **NEES** (Normalized Estimation Error Squared) — chi-squared consistency test
> - **NIS** (Normalized Innovation Squared) — for filter consistency
> - **Success Rate (SR)** — percentage of successful trials
> - **SPL** (Success weighted by Path Length) — $[0, 1]$
> - **F-score / IoU** — for mapping and segmentation tasks
> - **FID / NLL** — for generative probabilistic models

Any review of a probabilistic robotics paper that does not identify which of these metrics the paper uses (and whether the use is appropriate) is considered **incomplete** by KTU examiner standards.

---

### 1.4 Module 4 Positioning in the PERAT863 Course

| Course Module | Typical Focus | Relation to Module 4 |
|---|---|---|
| Module 1 | Bayesian Filtering Recap (KF, EKF, PF) | Module 4 papers extend these filters |
| Module 2 | SLAM and Graph-Based Optimization | Module 4 papers often propose new SLAM backends |
| Module 3 | Deep Learning meets Probabilistic Robotics | Module 4 papers integrate both paradigms |
| **Module 4** | **Research Frontiers & Critique** | **Synthesis of all prior modules** |

> [!NOTE]
> Module 4 is **integrative and metacognitive** — it does not introduce new theory; instead, it demands that the student *consume* new theory (from literature) and *defend* it (via review + presentation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Research Paper Review

A KTU-quality paper review must be built on **three irreducible pillars**. Skipping any one of them reduces the review to a *summary*, which is **explicitly insufficient** for full marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars**
> 
> 1. **Pillar 1 — Comprehension (What does the paper say?)**
> 2. **Pillar 2 — Critique (Is what the paper says true, novel, and useful?)**
> 3. **Pillar 3 — Contextualization (How does the paper fit into the broader landscape?)**

---

### 2.2 Pillar 1 — Comprehension: Anatomy of a Probabilistic Robotics Research Paper

Every contemporary research paper in this domain follows a near-universal **IMRaD-plus-extensions** structure:

| Section | Purpose | What a Reviewer Must Extract |
|---|---|---|
| **Title & Abstract** | One-sentence distillation | Problem, method, headline result |
| **Introduction** | Motivation and gap | What is missing in prior work? Why does it matter? |
| **Related Work** | Landscape mapping | How does this paper *differ* from prior art? |
| **Methodology / System** | Technical core | The probabilistic model, the algorithm, the assumptions |
| **Theoretical Analysis** | Bounds, proofs, convergence guarantees | Assumptions needed for guarantees; are they realistic? |
| **Experiments** | Empirical validation | Datasets, baselines, metrics, ablations |
| **Discussion / Limitations** | Honest accounting of weaknesses | What does the paper admit is unsolved? |
| **Conclusion & Future Work** | Forward-looking claims | Are the future-work claims credible? |
| **Reproducibility Appendix** | Code release, hyperparameters | Is the result reproducible from the paper alone? |

> [!NOTE]
> A senior examiner expects the student presenter to **devote ~50% of presentation time** to the Methodology + Experiments sections, because that is where the *probabilistic contribution* lives.

---

### 2.3 Pillar 2 — Critique: The Six-Question Audit Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six-Question Audit (High-Yield KTU Framework)**
> 
> A complete review answers these six questions explicitly. Each question corresponds to a common place where marks are lost if left unanswered.

1. **What is the precise probabilistic formulation?** (e.g., Is it a Bayes filter, a particle filter, a Gaussian process, a diffusion model?) — *If you cannot write the posterior, you have not understood the paper.*
2. **What are the explicit and implicit assumptions?** (Gaussian noise, static environment, known data association, etc.)
3. **What is the claimed improvement, and over which baseline?** (e.g., 20% ATE reduction over ORB-SLAM3)
4. **Is the comparison *fair*?** (Same datasets? Same compute budget? Same hyperparameter tuning effort?)
5. **Is the result *statistically significant*?** (Single run vs. multiple seeds, error bars, p-values)
6. **Is the result *generalizable*?** (Tested on 1 dataset vs. many; simulated vs. real)

---

### 2.4 Pillar 3 — Contextualization: Mapping the Paper onto the Field

> [!NOTE]
> A presentation that does not position the paper relative to (a) the seminal textbook (Thrun, Burgard, Fox — *Probabilistic Robotics*, 2005), (b) recent SOTA benchmarks, and (c) open challenges identified by the community, will be marked as **"lacking depth"**.

The reviewer should be able to:

- **Cite at least 2–3 predecessor papers** that the reviewed work builds upon.
- **Cite at least 1–2 follow-up works** (if any) that have already cited or extended the paper.
- **Identify the venue tier** (e.g., *IEEE T-RO*, *IJRR*, *RSS*, *ICRA*, *IROS*, *NeurIPS*, *CoRL*, *ICLR*).
- **Identify the reproducibility status** (code released? open dataset? closed benchmark?).

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet for Module 4

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since Module 4 evaluates a student's ability to **interpret, not compute**, the "formulas" below are the **interpretive and evaluation primitives** a student must master. These are tested both in viva and in written reflection.

| \# | Element | What It Is | Why It Matters (Real-World Utility) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Posterior $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$** | Belief over robot state given measurements and controls | Core object of *every* probabilistic robotics paper; must be writable on demand |
| 2 | **Bayes Filter Recursion** $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | The general update rule | Cited (in spirit) by every SLAM / localization paper |
| 3 | **Monte Carlo Approximation** $bel(x_t) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta(x_t - x_t^{(i)})$ | Particle filter representation | Used in nearly all modern particle-filter-based papers |
| 4 | **ATE Definition** $\text{ATE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert \text{trans}(Q_i^{-1} P_i) \Vert^2}$ | Absolute trajectory error (m) | Standard SLAM benchmark metric |
| 5 | **NEES Consistency** $\varepsilon_t = (x_t - \hat{x}_t)^\top P_t^{-1} (x_t - \hat{x}_t)$ | Filter consistency check | Lower is better; chi-squared bounds used |
| 6 | **Information Gain / Mutual Information** $I(X; Z \mid \text{path})$ | Active perception / next-best-view metric | Common in active SLAM and exploration papers |
| 7 | **ELBO (for deep probabilistic models)** $\log p(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(z)}[\log p(x \mid z)] - \text{KL}(q(z) \Vert p(z))$ | Variational inference bound | Used in papers on neural SLAM, generative perception |
| 8 | **POMDP Tuple** $\langle S, A, O, T, O, R, \gamma \rangle$ | Planning under uncertainty formulation | Used in decision-theoretic planning papers |
| 9 | **Cohen's $d$ / p-value** | Statistical significance of improvement | Determines whether a paper's claim is *real* |
| 10 | **Citation Count (h-index context)** | Proxy for impact | Provides *context*, never a substitute for content |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: In industry R&D (e.g., autonomous driving teams at Cruise, Waymo, Tesla; SLAM teams at Boston Dynamics, ANYbotics; research labs at MIT, ETH, Oxford), the ability to *critically consume* a research paper is more valuable than the ability to publish one. The Module 4 exercise trains exactly this skill.

---

### 2.6 Presentation Best-Practice Principles (KTU Evaluated)

| Principle | Definition | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| **10-20-30 Rule** (Guy Kawasaki) | 10 slides, 20 min, 30 pt min font | Stuffing 40+ slides |
| **Figure-Centric Slides** | Each slide dominated by a figure or diagram | Wall-of-text slides |
| **One Idea Per Slide** | A single coherent claim per slide | Two or more ideas conflated |
| **Time Budgeting** | ~1–1.5 min per slide for a 15-min talk | Running out of time mid-experiment section |
| **Visible Math, Not Verbal Math** | Show key equations; do not speak LaTeX aloud | Speaking "sigma" without showing the equation |
| **Honest Citation of Weaknesses** | Pre-emptively raise limitations | Over-claiming results |

## 3. Step-by-Step Methodology, Analytical Matrix, and Evaluation Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Module 4 is **procedural and analytical** rather than purely mathematical. The "derivations" here are the **logical derivations** of (a) the review methodology, (b) the scoring rubric interpretation, and (c) the presentation construction algorithm. Each step is shown in full, with no truncation.

---

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Review Methodology Pipeline

**Step 1 — Paper Selection**

A student must select a paper from a **pre-approved list** (issued by the course coordinator) OR propose a paper that satisfies:

- Published in a **Q1-tier venue** (T-RO, IJRR, JFR, RSS, ICRA, IROS, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, CoRL, CVPR, ECCV).
- Published in the **last 5 years** (contemporaneity criterion).
- Has a **clearly identifiable probabilistic component** (not a pure deep-learning paper with no probability).
- Has **reproducible code or at least reproducible experiments** (preferred).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Rule**: A paper with no probabilistic component (e.g., a pure deterministic end-to-end learning paper) is **not eligible** for review in PERAT863. This is an automatic disqualification.

**Step 2 — First Read (Skim, 30–45 minutes)**

Objective: Extract the **skeleton**.

- Read title, abstract, conclusion, and all figure captions first.
- Identify the **headline result** (the single number or claim the paper is selling).
- Identify the **headline method** (one-sentence description of the proposed algorithm).
- Note any terms / acronyms you do not know and look them up *immediately*.

**Step 3 — Second Read (Deep, 3–5 hours)**

Objective: Build the **mathematical model**.

- Re-derive (on paper) the key equation. If you cannot derive it, you have not understood it.
- List **every assumption** explicitly. Mark which assumptions are standard and which are novel (and possibly questionable).
- Identify the **loss function** (if learning-based) or the **objective function** (if optimization-based).
- For filter-based papers, identify the **prediction and update steps** explicitly.

**Step 4 — Third Read (Critical, 2–3 hours)**

Objective: Build the **critique matrix**.

For each major claim of the paper, fill in the **Critique Matrix** (see Section 3.2).

**Step 5 — Cross-Reading (Comparative, 2–3 hours)**

Objective: Position the paper.

- Read at least **2 cited predecessor papers** to verify the "novelty" claim.
- Read at least **1 follow-up / citing paper** (via Google Scholar) to gauge reception.
- Read at least **1 competing approach** (the strongest baseline) to gauge fair comparison.

**Step 6 — Synthesis (1–2 hours)**

Objective: Produce the **review report** (typically 4–8 pages) and the **presentation slides** (10–15 slides).

---

### 3.2 The Critique Matrix (Analytical Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **central analytical tool** of Module 4. Every reviewed paper must be scored on each of these dimensions. The KTU examiner uses a similar rubric internally.

$$
\text{Score} = w_1 C_1 + w_2 C_2 + w_3 C_3 + w_4 C_4 + w_5 C_5 + w_6 C_6
$$

where

- $C_1$ = **Clarity of Problem Formulation** (out of 10)
- $C_2$ = **Rigor of Probabilistic Model** (out of 10)
- $C_3$ = **Quality of Experimental Validation** (out of 10)
- $C_4$ = **Statistical Significance** (out of 10)
- $C_5$ = **Reproducibility and Code Availability** (out of 10)
- $C_6$ = **Novelty and Impact** (out of 10)

and the **default weights** are:

$$
w_1 = 0.15, \quad w_2 = 0.20, \quad w_3 = 0.20, \quad w_4 = 0.15, \quad w_5 = 0.10, \quad w_6 = 0.20
$$

with the constraint

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i = 1.00
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these weights?** Probabilistic rigor ($w_2$), experimental quality ($w_3$), and novelty ($w_6$) are weighted highest (20% each) because these are the *core* contributions of a probabilistic robotics paper. Clarity ($w_1$) and statistical significance ($w_4$) are weighted 15% each. Reproducibility ($w_5$) is weighted 10% — important but increasingly taken for granted.

**Interpretation bands:**

$$
\text{Score} \in \begin{cases} [9.0, 10.0] & \text{Exceptional — venue-top candidate} \\ [7.5, 9.0) & \text{Very Strong — likely accepted at top venue} \\ [6.0, 7.5) & \text{Acceptable — borderline, significant limitations} \\ [4.5, 6.0) & \text{Weak — major revision required} \\ [0.0, 4.5) & \text{Reject — fundamental flaws} \end{cases}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Applying the Critique Matrix

Suppose a student reviews a hypothetical paper **"Diffusion-PF: A Diffusion-Model-Augmented Particle Filter for Robust SLAM in Cluttered Environments"** (venue: *IEEE ICRA 2024*).

**Step 1 — Populate the $C_i$ scores:**

| Dimension | $C_i$ (out of 10) | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ — Clarity | **8** | Problem is well-motivated with clear figures; the SLAM-with-clutter framing is precise |
| $C_2$ — Probabilistic Rigor | **7** | Posterior is correctly derived; diffusion model integration is novel but the KL term lacks justification |
| $C_3$ — Experimental Quality | **8** | Tested on KITTI, EuRoC, and a custom dataset; real + simulated |
| $C_4$ — Statistical Significance | **5** | Only 3 seeds reported; no confidence intervals; p-values absent |
| $C_5$ — Reproducibility | **9** | Code released on GitHub; hyperparameters provided |
| $C_6$ — Novelty / Impact | **7** | Combination of diffusion + PF is novel; impact likely medium (3 follow-up citations as of review date) |

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score} &= (0.15 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) \\
&= 1.20 + 1.40 + 1.60 + 0.75 + 0.90 + 1.40 \\
&= 7.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the band:**

$$
7.25 \in [6.0, 7.5) \Rightarrow \text{Acceptable — borderline, significant limitations}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion drawn in the review**: "Diffusion-PF is a *promising* contribution that meaningfully extends the particle-filter SLAM family by integrating a learned likelihood. However, the absence of statistical significance reporting (multiple seeds, confidence intervals) prevents a definitive claim of superiority over the strongest baseline (e.g., ORB-SLAM3). The reproducibility of the code release is commendable. **Score: 7.25 / 10. Recommendation: Accept with major revision on the experimental section.**"

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Constructing the Presentation

> [!NOTE]
> The presentation construction follows a strict 5-stage algorithm. Each stage has a deliverable, a time budget, and a quality check.

**Stage 1 — The 5-Slide Skeleton (1 hour)**

Produce exactly these 5 slides first, before doing anything else:

1. **Title** — Paper title, authors, venue, your name, your affiliation.
2. **Motivation** — One figure + one sentence: *Why does this problem matter?*
3. **Headline Result** — One figure or one big number: *What did the paper achieve?*
4. **One-Sentence Method** — A single sentence + one block diagram.
5. **Bottom Line** — Your overall score + your recommendation in one sentence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If you cannot fill these 5 slides in 1 hour, you have not yet understood the paper. Go back to Step 2 (deep read).

**Stage 2 — The Methodology Block (2 hours)**

Build **3–4 slides** explaining the proposed probabilistic model:

- Slide 1: The **problem as a probabilistic graphical model** (PGM).
- Slide 2: The **prediction step** (motion model).
- Slide 3: The **update step** (measurement model).
- Slide 4 (if applicable): The **learning component** (loss function, training data, architecture).

> [!NOTE]
> A Mermaid or LaTeX-TikZ PGM (drawn on a slide) is a high-impact visual. Examiners reward visible rigor.

**Stage 3 — The Experiments Block (2 hours)**

Build **3–4 slides** on experiments:

- Slide 1: **Datasets and Baselines** (a clean table).
- Slide 2: **Headline quantitative result** (a bar chart or a trajectory-overlay figure).
- Slide 3: **Ablation study** (which component contributes how much?).
- Slide 4: **Qualitative result** (a video clip, if allowed; or a representative figure).

**Stage 4 — The Critique Block (1 hour)**

Build **2–3 slides** on critique:

- Slide 1: **Strengths** (3 bullets).
- Slide 2: **Weaknesses** (3 bullets).
- Slide 3: **Score and Recommendation** (use the critique matrix; be honest).

**Stage 5 — The Q&A Anticipation Block (1 hour)**

Prepare **5 likely Q&A questions** and pre-rehearse answers:

1. "What is the key assumption that the paper makes?"
2. "How does this compare to [baseline] under matched compute?"
3. "Did the authors release code? Did you verify it?"
4. "What is the failure mode?"
5. "What would be a natural follow-up to this work?"

> [!WARNING]
> A student who has not pre-rehearsed at least 3 Q&A answers is **unprepared** for the KTU viva portion.

---

### 3.5 Quality Check Equations for Presentation

Two self-imposed quality checks the student should run:

**Check 1 — The "Grandmother Test"**

$$
T_{\text{grandmother}} = \text{Number of slides a non-expert can understand} \geq \frac{2}{3} \times N_{\text{slides}}
$$

If less than two-thirds of the slides are comprehensible to a non-expert, the talk is **too technical**.

**Check 2 — The "1-Month-Later Recall Test"**

$$
R_{\text{recall}} = \text{Number of key claims you can still state after 1 month} \geq 3
$$

If you cannot remember 3 key claims one month later, the talk failed to communicate.
