## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below model the **exact style** of KTU 2024 Scheme assessments: a mix of Part A (short, 3 marks) and Part B (long, 14 marks) with internal choice. Each long question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. **Model solutions are provided in full**, step-by-step, as a KTU examiner would write them on the answer script.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Define the term "contemporary research paper" in the context of Module 4 of PERAT863. What is the minimum eligibility criterion for a paper to qualify as a reviewable paper in this module?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **contemporary research paper** in the context of PERAT863 is defined as a peer-reviewed article that (i) is published within the last **5 years** in a Q1-tier venue relevant to robotics, machine learning, or computer vision (e.g., *IEEE T-RO*, *IJRR*, *RSS*, *ICRA*, *IROS*, *NeurIPS*, *ICML*, *CoRL*, *CVPR*); and (ii) has an explicit, identifiable **probabilistic component** (e.g., a Bayes filter, a particle filter, a probabilistic graphical model, a Bayesian neural network, or a variational inference step).
>
> The **minimum eligibility criterion** is the **explicit probabilistic component**: a pure deterministic end-to-end learning paper with no probability in the model formulation is **not eligible** for review in this module, even if it is published in a top venue. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**List the three irreducible pillars of a research paper review as defined in Module 4. Briefly explain why "summary alone" is insufficient.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **three irreducible pillars** are:
>
> 1. **Comprehension** — what the paper says (problem, method, results).
> 2. **Critique** — whether what the paper says is true, novel, and useful.
> 3. **Contextualization** — how the paper fits into the broader landscape.
>
> A *summary* addresses only Pillar 1 (Comprehension). It does not interrogate assumptions, does not test statistical significance, and does not position the paper relative to prior art or follow-up work. A review that omits Critique (Pillar 2) and Contextualization (Pillar 3) is **descriptive, not analytical**, and is therefore marked down significantly by the KTU examiner. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Six-Question Audit Framework** that is recommended in Module 4 for the critical review of a probabilistic robotics paper. For each of the six questions, give one concrete example of the kind of evidence a reviewer should look for in the paper. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Critique Matrix** to a hypothetical paper titled *"Graph-PF: A Graph-Neural-Network-Augmented Particle Filter for Multi-Robot SLAM"*. Suppose the scores you assign are: $C_1 = 8$, $C_2 = 9$, $C_3 = 7$, $C_4 = 6$, $C_5 = 8$, $C_6 = 8$. Compute the weighted total score using the default weights and interpret the recommendation band. Suggest two concrete improvements the authors could make. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) The Six-Question Audit Framework (7 marks):**

> The Six-Question Audit Framework is a structured checklist that ensures a comprehensive critical review. For each question, the required evidence is:
>
> 1. **What is the precise probabilistic formulation?** — *Evidence to look for*: an equation (preferably numbered, e.g., Eq. 3) explicitly stating the posterior $p(\cdot)$, the prior, and the likelihood. A reviewer should be able to write this equation on a blank sheet from memory after reading the paper.
>
> 2. **What are the explicit and implicit assumptions?** — *Evidence to look for*: a "Assumptions" subsection, or assumptions embedded in the choice of noise model (e.g., "we assume Gaussian observation noise with diagonal covariance"). Implicit assumptions (e.g., static environment, known data association) are often **not** stated; the reviewer must infer them.
>
> 3. **What is the claimed improvement, and over which baseline?** — *Evidence to look for*: a quantitative headline result (e.g., "20% lower ATE than ORB-SLAM3 on KITTI sequence 00"). The reviewer should record the exact number, the baseline, and the dataset.
>
> 4. **Is the comparison fair?** — *Evidence to look for*: same training data, same compute budget, same hyperparameter tuning effort, same evaluation protocol. If the baseline was undertrained, the comparison is unfair.
>
> 5. **Is the result statistically significant?** — *Evidence to look for*: multiple random seeds (≥ 5 is standard), confidence intervals, p-values, or chi-squared consistency checks. Single-run results are **not** statistically significant.
>
> 6. **Is the result generalizable?** — *Evidence to look for*: testing on multiple datasets (simulated + real), multiple environments (indoor + outdoor), and multiple robot platforms. A result that works only on one dataset is **not** generalizable. **(7 marks — 1 mark per question + 0.5 mark for the "evidence" example + 0.5 mark for the rationale of why it matters)**

---

**(b) Critique Matrix Computation (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Recall the weighted score formula:**
>
> $$\text{Score} = w_1 C_1 + w_2 C_2 + w_3 C_3 + w_4 C_4 + w_5 C_5 + w_6 C_6$$
>
> **Step 2 — Substitute the default weights and the given scores:**
>
> $$w_1 = 0.15, \quad w_2 = 0.20, \quad w_3 = 0.20, \quad w_4 = 0.15, \quad w_5 = 0.10, \quad w_6 = 0.20$$
>
> $$C_1 = 8, \quad C_2 = 9, \quad C_3 = 7, \quad C_4 = 6, \quad C_5 = 8, \quad C_6 = 8$$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute each term:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> w_1 C_1 &= 0.15 \times 8 = 1.20 \\
> w_2 C_2 &= 0.20 \times 9 = 1.80 \\
> w_3 C_3 &= 0.20 \times 7 = 1.40 \\
> w_4 C_4 &= 0.15 \times 6 = 0.90 \\
> w_5 C_5 &= 0.10 \times 8 = 0.80 \\
> w_6 C_6 &= 0.20 \times 8 = 1.60 \\
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Sum:**
>
> $$\text{Score} = 1.20 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 1.60 = 7.70$$
>
> **Step 5 — Interpret the band:**
>
> $$7.70 \in [7.5, 9.0) \Rightarrow \text{Very Strong — likely accepted at top venue}$$
>
> **Step 6 — Two concrete improvements for the authors:**
>
> 1. **Improve statistical significance reporting** ($C_4 = 6$ is the weakest dimension): run all experiments with at least **5–10 random seeds**, report **mean ± standard deviation**, and include a **paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank test** against the strongest baseline.
> 2. **Broaden experimental evaluation** ($C_3 = 7$ is below the rigor ceiling): add at least **one outdoor real-world dataset** (e.g., KITTI) and **one multi-robot scenario** to demonstrate the claimed multi-robot contribution empirically, not just architecturally. **(7 marks — 2 marks for formula recall, 3 marks for computation, 1 mark for band interpretation, 1 mark for two concrete improvements)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]`

**(a)** Construct the **methodology block** of a 15-minute presentation reviewing a contemporary paper on **Visual-Inertial SLAM with Neural Network-Based Uncertainty Estimation**. Specify the **exact 4 slides** you would use, the **visual element** (figure / equation / table) on each slide, and the **time budget** for each slide. Justify your time allocation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of single-run experimental reporting** in probabilistic robotics papers. Why is this a pervasive problem? Provide **two statistical tests** that reviewers should demand authors include, and explain how each test answers a different question. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) The 4-Slide Methodology Block (7 marks):**

> The methodology block of a 15-minute presentation on *Visual-Inertial SLAM with Neural Network-Based Uncertainty Estimation* should consist of exactly 4 slides, distributed as follows:
>
> | Slide \# | Title | Visual Element | Time |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | M1 | **Probabilistic Graphical Model** | A hand-drawn or TikZ PGM showing camera, IMU, and uncertainty nodes | 1.5 min |
> | M2 | **Prediction Step (IMU-driven motion model)** | Equation: $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and a block diagram of the EKF propagation | 1.5 min |
> | M3 | **Update Step (Camera + NN uncertainty head)** | Equation: $p(z_t \mid x_t)$ and a small architectural diagram of the CNN that predicts per-pixel uncertainty | 1.0 min |
> | M4 | **Loss Function for Uncertainty Learning** | Equation: $\mathcal{L} = \Vert z - \hat{z} \Vert^2 \cdot \sigma^{-2} + \log \sigma^2$ and a training-curve figure | 1.0 min |
>
> **Total time**: 5.0 min (which is **one-third** of the 15-min talk, matching the recommended pie-chart allocation in Section 4.4).
>
> **Justification of time allocation**: Slides M1 and M2 are weighted more heavily (1.5 min each) because (i) M1 establishes the *probabilistic contract* of the paper (essential for examiner confidence), and (ii) M2 contains the motion model, which is the *engine* of any visual-inertial filter. Slides M3 and M4 are weighted less (1.0 min each) because M3 is a *standard* camera update step (audience familiarity is high) and M4 is a *specific* learning detail (audience familiarity is lower, but the slide itself is dense). **(7 marks — 1 mark per slide, 2 marks for the total-time justification, 1 mark for the proportionality argument)**

---

**(b) Limitations of Single-Run Reporting and Required Statistical Tests (7 marks):**

> **The Pervasive Problem of Single-Run Reporting**
>
> Single-run experimental reporting — i.e., reporting one number (e.g., ATE = 0.12 m) from one execution of the algorithm — is the **norm** in much of the robotics literature, but is **statistically meaningless**. The reported number is a *point estimate* with no uncertainty quantification, so the reader cannot tell whether the algorithm is genuinely better than the baseline or whether the result is a lucky seed. This problem is pervasive in probabilistic robotics because (i) experiments are expensive (real-robot runs take hours or days), (ii) the community has historically tolerated point estimates, and (iii) reviewers often do not enforce seed reporting.
>
> **Two Statistical Tests That Reviewers Should Demand**
>
> 1. **Paired t-test** (or **Wilcoxon signed-rank test** for non-Gaussian distributions): This tests the **null hypothesis** that the mean performance of the proposed method equals the mean performance of the strongest baseline. The output is a *p-value*. If $p < 0.05$, the difference is conventionally considered statistically significant. *This test answers the question*: "Is the proposed method *reliably* better than the baseline, or could the observed difference be due to chance?"
>
> 2. **Bootstrap Confidence Interval (e.g., 95% CI on ATE)**: This resamples the experimental runs (with replacement) thousands of times and reports the interval within which the true mean ATE lies with 95% probability. *This test answers the question*: "How much would the reported ATE vary if we re-ran the experiment?" — i.e., it quantifies the *spread* of the result, not just its *central tendency*.
>
> Together, the two tests provide: (i) a **significance verdict** (p-value) and (ii) a **magnitude estimate with uncertainty** (CI). A rigorous reviewer should demand both. **(7 marks — 2 marks for the pervasiveness argument, 2.5 marks for Test 1 (with null hypothesis), 2.5 marks for Test 2 (with resampling logic))**

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Module 4 (PERAT863)**
>
> 1. **Treating the review as a summary** — This is the **single most common pitfall**. A summary earns at most 30–40% of the marks. The remaining 60–70% comes from **critique and contextualization** (Pillars 2 and 3). If your review does not explicitly raise weaknesses, you will not score above a 6/10.
>
> 2. **Failing to write the posterior** — A review that cannot produce the paper's posterior equation on demand signals a surface-level read. Examiners will ask you to do this in viva. Practice deriving the equation from memory.
>
> 3. **Single-run reporting accepted uncritically** — Students frequently present papers' headline numbers without questioning their statistical significance. This is read by the examiner as "uncritical acceptance" and is **heavily penalized**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the assumption set** — Papers in this field often have **hidden assumptions** (static environment, known data association, Gaussian noise, perfect calibration). A review that does not surface these is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Over-claiming the novelty** — "This is the first paper to…" is a common claim; it is often false. A KTU examiner checks. A review that does not verify novelty against a quick Scholar search is vulnerable.
>
> 6. **Wall-of-text slides and missing time budget** — A 15-minute talk that runs to 25 minutes is an automatic quality deduction. Rehearse with a timer.
>
> 7. **Not anticipating Q&A** — A viva-style follow-up question on "What is the failure mode?" or "Did the authors release code?" is **expected**. A student who cannot answer forfeits substantial marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**

- [ ] A *contemporary research paper* in PERAT863 is a **Q1-venue paper from the last 5 years** with an **explicit probabilistic component**.
- [ ] The **three pillars** of a review are **Comprehension, Critique, Contextualization** — never just summary.
- [ ] The **Six-Question Audit** is the minimum critical tool: formulation, assumptions, gain, fairness, significance, generalizability.
- [ ] The **Critique Matrix** uses six scores $C_1$ through $C_6$ with weights $(0.15, 0.20, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10, 0.20)$.
- [ ] The **weighted sum** maps to a **band**: $\geq 9$ exceptional, $[7.5, 9)$ very strong, $[6, 7.5)$ acceptable, $[4.5, 6)$ weak, $< 4.5$ reject.
- [ ] **Standard metrics** in probabilistic robotics papers: **ATE, RPE, NEES, NIS, SR, SPL, IoU, F-score, NLL, FID** — recognize each.
- [ ] **Statistical significance** demands $\geq 5$ seeds, confidence intervals, and a **p-value** or **chi-squared test**.
- [ ] The **presentation pipeline** has **5 stages** (Skim → Deep → Critical → Cross → Synthesize) and a **6-stage construction** (5-skeleton → methodology → experiments → critique → Q&A → rehearsal).
- [ ] **Time budget** for a 15-min talk: 2 min motivation, 1 min headline, 5 min methodology, 4 min experiments, 2 min critique, 1 min Q&A.
- [ ] **Reproducibility** is non-negotiable: a paper without released code or with insufficient hyperparameter detail is downgraded on $C_5$.
- [ ] **Cite the seminal reference**: Thrun, Burgard, Fox — *Probabilistic Robotics* (2005) — for any foundational claim.
- [ ] **The "Grandmother Test"**: at least $\frac{2}{3}$ of slides should be comprehensible to a non-expert.
- [ ] **The "1-Month-Later Recall Test"**: at least **3 key claims** must remain memorable.
- [ ] **Rehearse Q&A** with at least **5 anticipated questions** before the viva.
- [ ] **Never accept a single-run result** at face value — always probe seed count and variance.
- [ ] **Always write the posterior** from memory after reading the paper.
